questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
Match List-I with List-II. 

 List-I  List-II  A.  Coefficient of viscosity  I. ML2 T-2 B.  Surface Tension  II. ML2 T-1 C.  Angular momentum  III. ML-1 T-1 D.  Rotational kinetic energy  IV. ML0 T-2
questionIds.solution:
Viscous force is given by, F=ηAdvdy.

Hence, MLT-2=ηL2T-1

⇒η=ML-1T-1

Surface tension is given by, ST=Fℓ.

Hence, dimension will be =MLT-2[L]=ML0T-2

Angular momentum, L=mvr=ML2T-1

Rotational kinetic energy, K.E=12Iω2=ML2T-2

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A liquid column of height $0.04 \mathrm{~cm}$ balances excess pressure of a soap bubble of certain radius. If density of liquid is $8 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$ and surface tension of soap solution is $0.28 \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}$, then diameter of the soap bubble is _____ $\mathrm{cm}$. (if $g=10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$ )
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \rho \mathrm{gh}=\frac{4 \mathrm{~S}}{\mathrm{R}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=\frac{4 \times 0.28}{8 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{0.28}{8} \mathrm{~m}=\frac{28}{8} \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=3.5 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$
Diameter $=7 \mathrm{~cm}$

ρ=EAi=EAneAvd
Drift velocity can be written as vd=eEmeτ
ρ=EAmeneAeEτ=mene2τ
=9.1×10-318.5×1028×1.6×10-192×25×10-15
≈1.65×10-8 Ω m

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.
Assertion A : Electromagnets are made of soft iron.
Reason R : Soft iron has high permeability and low retentivity.
In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> is not correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is correct

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> is correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is not correct

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> are correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math>
questionIds.solution:
A coil surrounding a magnetic core having high permeability and low retentivity, through which an electric current is fed to magnetise the core is what makes up an electromagnet. Wherever controllable magnets are needed, as in devices where the magnetic flux needs to be adjusted, reversed, or turned on and off, an electromagnet is used.

Hence, both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A.

questionIds.subject: Physics

questionIds.q:
A rod of length 5 L is bent right angle keeping one side length as 2 L.

The position of the centre of mass of the system: (Consider $\mathrm{L}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$)
questionIds.solution:
$\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{com}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~m}(10)+3 \mathrm{~m}(0)}{5 \mathrm{~m}}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{com}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~m}(0)+3 \mathrm{~m}(15)}{5 \mathrm{~m}}=9 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{com}}=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+9 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$

⇒ 2sinA2cosA2=3sinA2
⇒cosA2=32⇒A2=30°

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Two sources of light emit with a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. The ratio of number of photons of visible light emitted by each source having wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> respectively, will be :
questionIds.solution:
Let n1 and n2 be the number of photons coming out from the first and the second source of light respectively per second.

So, for the first source,

n1×hcλ1=200   ...1

And, for the second source,

n2×hcλ2=200   ...2

From equations (1) and (2), it implies that

n1n2=λ1λ2=300500⇒n1n2=35

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: In forced oscillation of a particle the amplitude is maximum for a frequency $\omega_1$ of the force, while the energy is maximum for a frequency $\omega_2$ of the force, then
options: $\omega_1 < \omega_2$ when damping is small and $\omega_1>\omega_2$ when damping is large
questionIds.solution:
Both amplitude and energy get maximised when the frequency is equal to the natural frequency. This is the condition of resonance.
$$
\omega_1=\omega_2
$$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Two wires of same length and radius are joined end to end and loaded. The Young's moduli of the materials of the two wires are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Y</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The combination behaves as a single wire then its Young's modulus is:
questionIds.solution:
In series combination, Δl=ℓ1+ℓ2

Y=F/AΔℓ/ℓ⇒Δℓ=FℓAY

⇒Δℓ∝ℓY

Equivalent length of rod after joining is =2ℓ

As, lengths are same and force is also same in series

Δℓ=Δℓ1+Δℓ2

ℓeqYeq=ℓY1+ℓY2⇒2ℓY=ℓY1+ℓY2

∴ Y=2Y1Y2Y1+Y2

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A three coulomb charge moves from the point $(0, -2, -5)$ to the point $(5, 1, 2)$ in an electric field expressed as $\vec{E} = 2x\hat{i} + 3y^2\hat{j} + 4\hat{k}$ N/C. The work done in moving the charge is _______ J.
questionIds.solution:
The force experienced by the charge in the electric field is given by $\vec{F} = q\vec{E}$.

Given $q = 3$ C and $\vec{E} = 2x\hat{i} + 3y^2\hat{j} + 4\hat{k}$ N/C, the force is:
$\vec{F} = 3(2x\hat{i} + 3y^2\hat{j} + 4\hat{k}) = 6x\hat{i} + 9y^2\hat{j} + 12\hat{k}$ N

The work done by the electric field in moving the charge is:
$W = \int \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{r} = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F_x dx + \int_{y_1}^{y_2} F_y dy + \int_{z_1}^{z_2} F_z dz$

Substituting the limits from the initial point $(0, -2, -5)$ to the final point $(5, 1, 2)$:
$W = \int_{0}^{5} 6x dx + \int_{-2}^{1} 9y^2 dy + \int_{-5}^{2} 12 dz$

Evaluating each integral separately:
$\int_{0}^{5} 6x dx = \left[ 3x^2 \right]_{0}^{5} = 3(5^2 - 0^2) = 75$ J

$\int_{-2}^{1} 9y^2 dy = \left[ 3y^3 \right]_{-2}^{1} = 3(1^3 - (-2)^3) = 3(1 - (-8)) = 3(9) = 27$ J

$\int_{-5}^{2} 12 dz = \left[ 12z \right]_{-5}^{2} = 12(2 - (-5)) = 12(7) = 84$ J

Total work done $W = 75 + 27 + 84 = 186$ J.

Answer: $186$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: An engine approaches a hill with a constant speed. When it is at a distance of $0.9 \mathrm{~km}$, it blows a whistle whose echo is heard by the driver after $5$ seconds. If the speed of sound in air is $330 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, then the speed of the engine is :
questionIds.solution: Let after $5 \mathrm{~sec}$ engine at point $\mathrm{C}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{t}=\frac{\mathrm{AB}}{330}+\frac{\mathrm{BC}}{330} \\ & 5=\frac{0.9 \times 1000}{330}+\frac{\mathrm{BC}}{330} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{BC}=750 \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned}$ Distance travelled by engine in $5 \mathrm{~sec}$ $=900 \mathrm{~m}-750 \mathrm{~m}=150 \mathrm{~m}$ Therefore velocity of engine $=\frac{150 \mathrm{~m}}{5 \mathrm{~sec}}=30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Moment of inertia about an axis $AB$ for a rod of mass $40$ kg and length $3$ m is same as that of a solid sphere of mass of $10$ kg and radius $R$ about an axis parallel to $AB$ axis with separation of $3$ m as shown in figure below. The value of $R$ is given as $\sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha}{2}}$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
questionIds.solution:
Moment of inertia of the rod about axis $AB$ passing through one of its ends:
$I_{\text{rod}} = \dfrac{ML^2}{3} = \dfrac{40 \times (3)^2}{3} = \dfrac{40 \times 9}{3} = 120\text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$

Moment of inertia of the solid sphere about the same axis $AB$, using the parallel axis theorem $I = I_{\text{cm}} + md^2$:
Mass $m = 10\text{ kg}$, radius $R$, distance from the centre of the sphere to axis $AB$ is $d = 3\text{ m}$.

$I_{\text{sphere}} = \dfrac{2}{5}mR^2 + md^2 = \dfrac{2}{5}(10)R^2 + 10(3)^2 = 4R^2 + 90$

Equating the two moments of inertia:
$120 = 4R^2 + 90$
$4R^2 = 30$
$R^2 = \dfrac{15}{2}$
$R = \sqrt{\dfrac{15}{2}}$

Comparing with $R = \sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha}{2}}$:
$\alpha = 15$

Hence, $\alpha = 15$.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
The energy released in the fusion of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ of hydrogen deep in the sun is $E_H$ and the energy released in the fission of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U}$ is $E_U$. The ratio $\frac{E_H}{E_U}$ is approximately:
(Consider the fusion reaction as $4 \mid H+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow{ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}+2 v+6 \gamma+26.7 \mathrm{MeV}$, energy released in the fission reaction of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U}$ is $200 \mathrm{MeV}$ per fission nucleus and $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=$ $\left.6.023 \times 10^{23}\right)$
questionIds.solution:
In each fusion reaction, $4{ }_1^1 \mathrm{H}$ nucleus are used. 
Energy released per Nuclei of ${ }_1^1 \mathrm{H}=\frac{26.7}{4} \mathrm{MeV}$
$\therefore$ Energy released by $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ hydrogen $\left(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}}\right)$
$=\frac{2000}{1} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times \frac{26.7}{4} \mathrm{MeV}$
$\qquad \qquad\quad \&$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \text { Energy released by } 2 \mathrm{~kg} \text { Vranium }\left(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{V}}\right) \\ & =\frac{2000}{235} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times 200 \mathrm{MeV}\end{aligned}$
So,
$\frac{E_H}{E_V}=235 \times \frac{26.7}{4 \times 200}=7.84$
$\therefore$ Approximately close to 7.62

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
Given below are two statements :

Statement - I : Susceptibilities of paramagnetic and ferromagnetic substances increase with decrease in temperature.

Statement - II : Diamagnetism is a result of orbital motions of electrons developing magnetic moments opposite to the applied magnetic field.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement - I and Statement - II are true.
Both Statement - I and Statement - II are false.
Statement - I is true but Statement - II is false.
Statement- I is false but Statement - II is true.
questionIds.solution:
Statement-I is true as susceptibility of ferromagnetic and paramagnetic materials is inversely related to temperature, so it increases with decrease in temperature.

Statement-II is also true as because of orbital motion of electrons, the diamagnetic material is able to oppose external magnetic field. Once an external magnetic field is applied, diamagnetic materials develop an induced magnetic field opposite to this field and thus are repelled by the field.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
Student A and student B used two screw gauges of equal pitch and 100 equal circular divisions to measure the radius of a given wire. The actual value of the radius of the wire is 0.322 cm. The absolute value of the difference between the final circular scale readings observed by the students A and B is _______.

[Figure shows position of reference O when jaws of screw gauge are closed]

Given pitch =0.1 cm.
questionIds.solution:
For A,

Reading =MSR+CSR+Error

0.322=0.300+CSR+5×LC

0.322=0.300+CSR+0.005

CSR=0.017

For B, 

Reading =MSR+CSR+Error

0.322=0.200+CSR+0.092

CSR=0.030

Difference =0.030-0.017=0.013 cm

Division on circular scale =0.0130.001=13

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A thin prism of angle 6° and refractive index for yellow light nY1.5 is combined with another prism of angle 5° and nY=1.55. The combination produces no dispersion. The net average deviation δ produced by the combination is 1x°. The value of x is _____ .
questionIds.solution:
The formula for deviation due to a thin prism is given by, δ=Aμ-1.

Therefore, for prism 1:

δ1=61.5-1=3°

And for prism 2:

δ2=-51.55-1=-2.75°

⇒δnet=δ1+δ2=3°-2.75°=0.25°=14°

Therefore, x=4.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A current carrying circular loop of radius $2$ cm with unit normal $\hat{n} = \dfrac{\hat{k}+\hat{i}}{\sqrt{2}}$ is placed in a magnetic field, $\vec{B} = B_0(3\hat{i}+2\hat{k})$. If $B_0 = 4\times 10^{-3}$ T and current $I=100\sqrt{2}$ A, the torque experienced by the loop is ________ Wb·A. ($\pi=3.14$)
questionIds.solution:
The magnetic dipole moment of the current carrying loop is given by $\vec{M} = I A \hat{n}$.

Given radius $r = 2$ cm $= 2 \times 10^{-2}$ m, the area of the loop is:
$A = \pi r^2 = \pi (2 \times 10^{-2})^2 = 4\pi \times 10^{-4}$ m$^2$

Substituting the given values $I = 100\sqrt{2}$ A and $\hat{n} = \dfrac{\hat{i}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{2}}$:
$\vec{M} = (100\sqrt{2}) \times (4\pi \times 10^{-4}) \times \left(\dfrac{\hat{i}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$
$\vec{M} = 4\pi \times 10^{-2} (\hat{i}+\hat{k})$ A$\cdot$m$^2$

The torque experienced by the loop in the magnetic field is $\vec{\tau} = \vec{M} \times \vec{B}$.
Given $\vec{B} = 4 \times 10^{-3} (3\hat{i}+2\hat{k})$ T, we have:
$\vec{\tau} = [4\pi \times 10^{-2} (\hat{i}+\hat{k})] \times [4 \times 10^{-3} (3\hat{i}+2\hat{k})]$
$\vec{\tau} = 16\pi \times 10^{-5} [(\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \times (3\hat{i}+2\hat{k})]$

Evaluating the cross product:
$(\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \times (3\hat{i}+2\hat{k}) = (\hat{i} \times 3\hat{i}) + (\hat{i} \times 2\hat{k}) + (\hat{k} \times 3\hat{i}) + (\hat{k} \times 2\hat{k})$
$= 0 + 2(-\hat{j}) + 3(\hat{j}) + 0 = \hat{j}$

Therefore, the torque is:
$\vec{\tau} = 16\pi \times 10^{-5} \hat{j}$

Substituting $\pi = 3.14$:
$\vec{\tau} = 16 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-5} \hat{j} = 50.24 \times 10^{-5} \hat{j} = 5024 \times 10^{-7} \hat{j}$ Wb$\cdot$A

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questionIds.q: The reaction that does NOT define calcination is:
questionIds.solution:
Calcination involves heating of carbonates or hydrated oxides in absence of air.

$$

\therefore 2 \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} 2 \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{2} ; \text { is roasting }

$$

(heating in a regular supply of air).

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Ka1,Ka2 and Ka3 are the respective ionization constants for the following reactions a,b and c.

(a) H2C2O4⇌H++HC2O4-

(b) HC2O4-⇌H++HC2O42-

(c) H2C2O4⇌2H++C2O42-

The relationship between Ka1, Ka2 and Ka3 is given as
questionIds.solution:
H2C2O4⇌H++HC2O4-Ka1

H2C2O4-⇌H++C2O42-Ka2

H2C2O4⇌2H++C2O42-   Ka3

On adding equation 1 and equation 2 we get equation 3. So, Ka3=Ka1×Ka2.

Reason : Aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution reaction.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:

Both (A) and (R) true but (R) is not the correct

(A) is false but ( R) is true.
questionIds.solution: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questionIds.q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: $\quad \mathrm{C}<\mathrm{O}<\mathrm{N}<\mathrm{F}$ is the correct order in terms of first ionization enthalpy values.
Statement II: $\quad \mathrm{S}>\mathrm{Se}>\mathrm{Te}>\mathrm{Po}>\mathrm{O}$ is the correct order in terms of the magnitude of electron gain enthalpy values.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
questionIds.solution:
Statement I:
Ionization enthalpy generally increases across a period from left to right.
However, Nitrogen ($2s^2 2p^3$) has a stable half-filled electronic configuration, making it harder to remove an electron compared to Oxygen ($2s^2 2p^4$).
Thus, the order is $C < O < N < F$.
Statement I is true.

Statement II:
For Group 16 elements, Oxygen has an exceptionally low magnitude of electron gain enthalpy due to its small size and high inter-electronic repulsions in the $2p$ subshell.
The magnitude of electron gain enthalpy decreases down the group from Sulphur onwards.
The correct order of magnitude is $S > Se > Te > Po > O$.
Statement II is true.

Both statements are correct.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
In the following balanced reaction,
values of $X, Y$ and $Z$ respectively are
questionIds.solution:
$X \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+Y \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-}+Z \mathrm{H}^{+} \leftrightharpoons$
$$
X \mathrm{Mn}^{++}+2 Y \mathrm{CO}_2+\frac{Z}{2} \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
$$
First half reaction
$$
\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{++}
$$
On balancing
$$
\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+}+5 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{++}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
$$


Second half reaction
$$
\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2
$$
On balancing
$$
\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}
$$
On multiplying eqn. (ii) by 5 and (iv) by 2 and then adding we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+5 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{--}+16 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \\
& 2 \mathrm{Mn}^{++}+10 \mathrm{CO}_2+8 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: The increasing order of nitration of the following compounds is :
questionIds.solution:
In the given substituted benzene rings, the substitutents methoxy $\left(-\mathrm{OCH}_3\right)$ and amino $\left(-\mathrm{NH}_2\right)$ are strongly activating groups while methyl $\left(-\mathrm{CH}_3\right)$ is weakly activating and chloro $(-\mathrm{Cl})$ is a deactivating group towards electrophilic aromatic substituation reaction. Since among methyl and methoxy group, methoxy group is more reactive than methyl group, (c) is more reactive than (d). Although amino group is strongly activating group, it gets protonated in presence of acid to form anilinium ion
which is strongly deactivating. Hence, (a) is less reactive than (c) and (d). Chloro group is also deactivating group but less deactivating than
. Thus order is (a) $ < $ (b) $ < $ (d) $ < $ (c). Note: The activating groups increases the electron density on benzene ring and increases the rate of electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction. The deactivating groups decreases the electron density on benzene ring and decreases the rate of electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction.

questionIds.q: Which pair of oxides is acidic in nature?
questionIds.solution:
An oxide that combines with water to give an acid is termed as an acidic oxide. The oxide that gives a base in water is known as a basic oxide. An amphoteric solution is a substance that can chemically react as either acid or base.

B2O3 and SiO2 both are oxides of non-metal and hence are acidic in nature.

CaO,BaO=Basic NatureB2O3,SiO2=Acidic NatureN2O=Neutral oxide

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: The $\mathrm{pKa}$ of a weak acid $(\mathrm{HA})$ is $4.5$. The $\mathrm{pOH}$ of an aqueous buffered solution of $\mathrm{HA}$ in which $50 \%$ of the acid is ionized is
questionIds.solution:
For buffer solution
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]} \\
& =4.5+\log \frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]}
\end{aligned}$
as $\mathrm{HA}$ is $50 \%$ ionized so $[\text{Salt}]$=$[\text{Acid}]$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{pH}=4.5 \\
& \mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH}=14 \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{pOH}=14-4.5=9.5
\end{aligned}$
Hence (C) is correct.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A): In expensive scientific instruments, silica gel is kept in watch-glasses or in semipermeable membrane bags.

Reason (R): Silica gel adsorbs moisture from air via adsorption, thus protects the instrument from water corrosion (rusting) and / or prevents malfunctioning.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
questionIds.solution:
The moist air becomes dry in presence of silica gel because the water molecules present in air get adsorbed on the surface of the gel. Silica gel adsorbs moisture from air via adsorption, thus protects the instrument from water corrosion (rusting) and/or prevents malfunctioning.

So, both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: Which among the following has highest boiling point?
questionIds.solution: The boiling points of aldehydes and ketones are higher than hydrocarbons and ethers of comparable molecular masses. It is due to weak molecular association in aldehydes and ketones arising out of the dipole-dipole interactions. Also, their boiling points are lower than those of alcohols of similar molecular masses due to absence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The number of $3 \times 2$ matrices A, which can be formed using the elements of the set $\{-2,-1,0,1,2\}$ such that the sum of all the diagonal elements of $\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{A}$ is 5, is
questionIds.solution:
For a 3×2 matrix A, the diagonal sum of $A^T A$ equals the sum of squares of all 6 elements of A. We need:
$a_{11}^2 + a_{21}^2 + a_{31}^2 + a_{12}^2 + a_{22}^2 + a_{32}^2 = 5$ where each element is from $\{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}$.
Possible squares: $0^2=0$, $(\pm 1)^2=1$, $(\pm 2)^2=4$.
Case 1:
Distribution $4+1+0+0+0+0$ (one ±2, one ±1, four 0's).
Arrangements: $\binom{6}{1} \times \binom{5}{1} = 30$.
Sign choices: $2 \times 2 = 4$. Total: $30 \times 4 = 120$.
Case 2:
Distribution $1+1+1+1+1+0$ (five ±1, one 0).
Arrangements: $\binom{6}{1} = 6$ ways to place the 0.
Sign choices for five ±1: $2^5 = 32$. Total: $6 \times 32 = 192$.
Total matrices: $120 + 192 = 312$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The sum of the maximum and minimum values of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi>t</mi></mfenced></math> is the greatest integer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>≤</mo><mi>t</mi></math>, is ______.
questionIds.solution:
Given,

fx=5x-7+x2+2x

=5x-7+x+12-1

=5x-7+x+12-1

as x-1=x-1 where . is greatest integer function

Now critical points of

fx=75,5-1,6-1,7-1,8-1,2

∴ Maximum or minimum value of fx occur at critical points or boundary points.

So,  f54=34+4=194 and f75=0+4=4

As both 5x-7 and x2+2x are increasing in nature after x=75

So, f2=10-7+4+4=3+8=11

∴ f75min=4 and f2max=11

So sum of minimum and maximum value is 4+11=15

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The absolute difference of the coefficients of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>7</mn></msup></math> in the expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mn>11</mn></msup></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
The given expansion is 2x2+12x11.

The general term in the binomial expansion of x+an is given by Tr+1=Crnxn-rar.

⇒Tr+1=Cr112x211-r12xr

=Cr11211-r-rx22-2r-r

Now for coefficient of x7⇒22-3r=7

⇒r=5

Coefficient of x7 is C511211-5×2

Now for coefficient of x10⇒22-3r=10

⇒r=4

Coefficient of x10 is C411211-2×4

We need the absolute difference, we get it by C511211-5×2-C411211-4×2.

=924-2640=1716

=123-12

Hence the absolute difference is =123-12.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Two number $\mathrm{k}_1$ and $\mathrm{k}_2$ are randomly chosen from the set of natural numbers. Then, the probability that the value of $\mathrm{i}^{\mathrm{k}_1}+\mathrm{i}^{\mathrm{k}_2},(\mathrm{i}=\sqrt{-1})$ is non-zero, equals
questionIds.solution:
Let $k_1=4 \lambda_1+r_1, r_1 \in\{0,1,2,3\}$
$k_2=4 \lambda_2+r_2$
$\begin{aligned}
& (i)^{k_1}+(i)^{k_2}=(i)^{r_1}+(i)^{r_2} \\
& (i)^{r_1} \in\{1, i,-1,-i\}
\end{aligned}$
$\text { Zero } \Rightarrow 1,(-1) \text { pair } \quad \Rightarrow\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
1, & -1 \\
i, & -i \\
-i,+i \\
-1, & 1
\end{array}\right\}$
$i,(-i)$ pair
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Zero probability }=\frac{4}{{ }^4 C_1 \cdot{ }^4 C_1}=\frac{1}{4} \\ & \text { Probability (non-zero) }=1-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{3}{4}\end{aligned}$

questionIds.q: Let a vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">β</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> be obtained by rotating the vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> by an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> about the origin in counterclockwise direction in the first quadrant. Then the area (in sq. units) of triangle having vertices <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:

=12OA'2sin30°2

Now, OA=OA', since rotation will not change the magnitude.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The number of 3 -digit numbers, that are divisible by 2 and 3 , but not divisible by 4 and 9 , is______.
questionIds.solution:
No, of 3 digits $=999-99=900$
No. of 3 digit numbers divisible by 2 & 3 i.e. by 6
$\frac{900}{6}=150$

No. of 3 digit numbers divisible by 4 & 9 i.e. by 36
$\frac{900}{36}=25$
$\therefore$ No of 3 digit numbers divisible by $2 ~\&~ 3$ but not by $4 ~\&~ 9$
$150-25=125$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The coefficient of <math> <mrow> <msup> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>x</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>1</mn> <mn>0</mn> <mn>1</mn> <mn>2</mn> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math>  in the expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mi>n</mi></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>253</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mn>0</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>≤22</mo></math> is any positive integer), is
questionIds.solution:
Given 1+xn+x25310

=1+x253+xn10

Using the binomial expansion a+bn=C0nanb0+C1nan-1b+C2nan-2b2+...+Cnna0bn,

=10C01+x25310xn0+10C11+x2539xn1+10C21+x2538xn2+...+10C101+x2530xn10

As  2 5 3 = 2 3 × 1 1  and 1012=253×4, also n≤22

⇒ Coefficient of x1012 will come only from the first term, i.e. in

10C01+x25310xn0=1+x25310

The general term in the expansion of 1+an is Tr+1=Crnar

Hence, the general term in the expansion of 1+x25310 is Tr+1=Cr10x253r=Cr10x253r

Since, 1012=253×4, hence r=4

Thus, the required coefficient is=10C4.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> be two vectors such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>⋅</mo><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>c</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>×</mo><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>−</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and the angle between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>c</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>192</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to _________
questionIds.solution:
Given,

a→ and b→ be two vectors such that a→=1, b→=4 and a→⋅b→=2.

Also, given

c→=2a→×b→−3b→

Taking dot product with b→ both side we get,

⇒b→⋅c→=2a→×b→⋅b→−3b2

⇒b→c→cosα=0−3b→2

⇒c→cosα=−12 {as b→=4}

Now, solving a→⋅b→=2

⇒a→b→cosθ=2

⇒cosθ=12⇒θ=π3

Now, solving

c→2=2a→×b→−3b→2

⇒c→2=64×34+144=192

Now, squaring both side of c→cosα=−12 we get,

c→2cos2α=144

⇒192cos2α=144

⇒1921-sin2α=144

⇒192sin2α=48

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: A straight line through the point A(3, 4) is such that its intercept between the axes is bisected at A. Its equation is
questionIds.solution:
The equation of axes is $x y=0$
$\Rightarrow$ the equation of the line is $\frac{x \cdot 4+y \cdot 3}{2}=12 \Rightarrow 4 x+3 y=24$

Then the number of ordered pairs which satisfy this equivalence relation with ordered pair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to :
questionIds.solution:

a,b=c,d⇒ad=bc

⇒43=cd

cd=43 & c,d∈2,3,……,30

c, d =4,3,8,6,12,9,16,12,20,15,24,18,28,21

No. of ordered pair =7

questionIds.q: Let y=fx=sin3π3cosπ32-4x3+5x2+132. Then, at x=1,
questionIds.solution:
The given equation can be written as

where,

⇒g'x=π22-4x3+5x2+11/2-12x2+10x

⇒g'1=π222-2=-π

And,

g1=2π3=π-π3

Now,

y'=3sin2π3cosgx×cosπ3cosgx×π3-singxg'x

y'1=3sin2-π6·cosπ6·π3-sin2π3g'1

y'1=34·32·π3-32-π=3π216

y1=sin3π3cos2π3=-18

Therefore,

2y'1+3π2y1=0

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>∞</mo></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi></math> be defined by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Then the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math>
questionIds.solution:
fx=1xloge1+x, x≠0

f'x=x1+x-ln1+xx2=x-1+xln1+xx21+x

Let gx=x-1+xln1+x

g'x=-ln1+x

⇒g'x>0∀x∈-1,0<0∀x∈0,∞

gmax at x=0⇒g0=0 is the maximum value

So, gx<0∀x∈-1,∞  ⇒ f'x<0∀x∈-1,∞

Hence, fx is decreasing ∀x∈-1,∞

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>-</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{" separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mi>A</mi></math> defined by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> is surjective, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
A function is surjective, when the range of the function is same as the co-domain.

Now, y=x21-x2

⇒y-yx2=x2 

⇒y=x2y+1

⇒x2=yy+1

∵  x2≥0 ⇒yy+1≥0

⇒y ∈-∞, -1∪0, ∞ or R-−1, 0.

∴ Range of f=A=R-−1, 0.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The equation of the line passing through <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> parallel to the plane <math><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and intersecting the line <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mi> </mi><mo>+</mo><mi> </mi><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> is
questionIds.solution:
A variable point on line x + 1-3=y-32=z-2-1 is -3t-1, 2t+3,-t+2

∴DR of variable point & -4, 3, 1 is

-3t+3, 2t, -t+1

Since line is parallel to x+2y-z-5=0

∴-3t+3+4t+t-1=0

⇒t=-1

∴DR of line is 6,-2, 2 or 3, -1, 1

∴ Equation of line is x+43=y-3-1=z-11

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The common difference of the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mo>.</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>,</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> more than common difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mo>.</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msub></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mn>40</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>159</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mn>100</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>399</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mn>100</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mn>70</mn></msub></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is equal to :
questionIds.solution:
Let a1,a1+d1,a1+2d.................first A.P.

a40=a1+39d=-159.....................(1)

a100=a1+99d=-399....................(2)

d=-4,a1=-3

now

b100=a70

⇒b1+99D=a1+69d

b1+99(-2)=-3+69(-4)

(∵Accoding to question D=d+2)

⇒b1=-81

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: A circle cuts a chord of length 4 a on the $x$ -axis and passes through a point on the $y$ -axis, distant $2 \mathrm{~b}$ from the origin. Then the locus of the centre of this circle, is:
questionIds.solution: Let centre be $C(h, k)$ $C Q=C P=r$ $\Rightarrow C Q^{2}=C P^{2}$ $(h-0)^{2}+(k \pm 0)^{2}=C M^{2}+M P^{2}$ $h^{2}+(k \pm 2 \mathrm{~b})^{2}=k^{2}+4 a^{2}$ $h^{2}+k^{2}+4 b^{2} \pm 4 b k=k^{2}+4 a^{2}$ Then, the locus of centre $C(h, k)$ $x^{2}+4 b^{2} \pm 4 b y=4 a^{2}$ Hence, the above locus of the centre of circle is a parabola.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If $f(x)$ satisfies the relation $f(x)=e^{x}+\int_{0}^{1}\left(y+x e^{x}\right) f(y) d y$, then $e+f(0)$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
questionIds.solution:
$f(x) = e^x + \displaystyle\int_0^1 (y + xe^x)f(y)\, dy$

Splitting the integral:
$f(x) = e^x + \displaystyle\int_0^1 yf(y)\, dy + xe^x \int_0^1 f(y)\, dy$

Let $A = \displaystyle\int_0^1 yf(y)\, dy$ and $B = \displaystyle\int_0^1 f(y)\, dy$

$\Rightarrow f(x) = (1 + Bx)e^x + A$

At $x = 0$: $f(0) = 1 + A$

Now compute $B$ using the expression for $f(y)$:
$B = \displaystyle\int_0^1 \left[(1 + By)e^y + A\right] dy$
$B = \displaystyle\int_0^1 e^y\, dy + B\int_0^1 ye^y\, dy + \int_0^1 A\, dy$

Evaluating each integral:
$\displaystyle\int_0^1 e^y\, dy = e - 1$
$\displaystyle\int_0^1 ye^y\, dy = [ye^y]_0^1 - \int_0^1 e^y\, dy = e - (e - 1) = 1$
$\displaystyle\int_0^1 A\, dy = A$

$\Rightarrow B = (e - 1) + B(1) + A$
$\Rightarrow 0 = e - 1 + A$
$\Rightarrow A = 1 - e$

$f(0) = 1 + A = 1 + (1 - e) = 2 - e$

$e + f(0) = e + (2 - e) = 2$

Answer: $2$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mi>β</mi><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>c</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></math> be three vectors. If the projection of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>c</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> and<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>×</mo><mover><mi>c</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> , then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></math> equal to
questionIds.solution:
Given a→=αi^+3j^-k^,b→=3i^-βj^+4k^ and c→=i^+2j^-2k^ where α,β∈R be three vectors.

Projection of a→ on c→ is a→·c→c→=103 (given)

⇒α+6+21+4+4=103⇒α=2

and b→×c→=i^j^k^3-β412-2=-6i^+j^+k^

⇒2β-8=-6⇒β=1

Hence α+β=3

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>6</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> is :
questionIds.solution:
fx=3sin4x+10sin3x+6sin2x-3, x∈-π6,π2

⇒f'x=12sin3xcosx+30sin2xcosx+12sinxcosx

=6sinxcosx2sin2x+5sinx+2

=6sinxcosx(2sinx+1)(sinx+2)

From wavy curve, we can say f'(x)<0 in -π6,0

So, the function is Decreasing in -π6,0

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The number of matrices of order <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>3</mn></math>, whose entries are either <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> and the sum of all the entries is a prime number, is _______.
questionIds.solution:
Let the matrix be abcdefghi

Since the elements can be either of 0 or 1, so

a+b+c+d+e+f+g+h+i=2/3/5/7

For sum as 2, the number of cases will be 9!2!7!=36

For sum as 3, the number of cases will be 9!3!6!=84

For sum as 5, the number of cases will be 9!5!4!=126

For sum as 7, the number of cases will be 9!2!7!=36

Total required matrix will be 282

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>t</mi></mfenced></math> denote the greatest integer less than or equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math>. Then the value of the integral <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow><mn>101</mn></msubsup><mfenced><mrow><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>π</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Given, ∫-3101sinπx+ecos2πxdx

Now let I1=∫-3101sinπxdx and I2=∫-3101ecos2πxdx

Now checking periodicity of sinπx we get,

0<x<1⇒sinπx=0

1<x<2⇒sinπx=-1

sinπx→ periodic with period 2

So, I1=52∫02sinπxdx

=52∫010+∫12-1dx=-52

Now checking periodicity of cos2πx we get,

0<x<14⇒cos2πx=0

14<x<12⇒cos2πx=-1

12<x<34⇒cos2πx=-1

34<x<1⇒cos2πx=0

So, cos2πx has periodicity of 1

So, I2=104∫014e0dx+∫1434e-1dx+∫341e0dx

⇒I2=10414+1e×12+14

⇒I2=52+52e

So, I1+I2=52e or ∫-3101sinπx+ecos2πxdx=52e

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>+</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>.</mo></math> Then, <math><mi>f</mi></math> is an increasing function in the interval:
questionIds.solution:
fx=sin4⁡x+cos4⁡x

f′x=4sin3⁡xcos⁡x+4cos3⁡x-sin⁡x

=4sin⁡xcos⁡xsin2⁡x-cos2⁡x

= -2sin⁡2xcos⁡2x

= -sin⁡4x

fx, is increasing when f′x>0

⇒ -sin⁡4x>0

⇒ sin⁡4x<0

⇒π<4x<2π

⇒π4<x<π2
⇒ x ∈π4,π2

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let $y^2=12 x$ be the parabola and $S$ be its focus. Let PQ be a focal chord of the parabola such that $(\mathrm{SP})(\mathrm{SQ})=\frac{147}{4}$. Let C be the circle described taking PQ as a diameter. If the equation of a circle $C$ is $64 x^2+64 y^2-\alpha x-64 \sqrt{3} y=\beta$, then $\beta-\alpha$ is equal to ________.
questionIds.solution:
$\mathrm{y}^2=12 \mathrm{x} \quad \mathrm{a}=3 \quad \mathrm{SP} \times \mathrm{SQ}=\frac{147}{4}$

Let $\mathrm{P}\left(3 \mathrm{t}^2, 6 \mathrm{t}\right)$ and $\mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_2=-1$
(ends of focal chord)
So, $Q\left(\frac{3}{t^2}, \frac{-6}{t}\right)$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{S}(3,0) \\
& \mathrm{SP} \times \mathrm{SQ}=\mathrm{PM}_1 \times \mathrm{QM}_2
\end{aligned}$
(dist. from directrix)
$\begin{aligned}
& =\left(3+3 \mathrm{t}^2\right)\left(3+\frac{3}{\mathrm{t}^2}\right)=\frac{147}{4} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{\left(1+\mathrm{t}^2\right)^2}{\mathrm{t}^2}=\frac{49}{12} \\
& \mathrm{t}^2=\frac{3}{4}, \frac{4}{3} \\
& \mathrm{t}= \pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \pm \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}
\end{aligned}$
considering $\mathrm{t}=\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{9}{4},-3 \sqrt{3}\right) \text { and } \mathrm{Q}(4,4 \sqrt{3})$

Hence, diametric circle:
$\begin{aligned}
& (x-4)\left(x-\frac{9}{4}\right)+(y+3 \sqrt{3})(y-4 \sqrt{3})=0 \\
& \Rightarrow x^2+y^2-\frac{25}{4} x-\sqrt{3} y-27=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \alpha=400, \beta=1728 \\
& \beta-\alpha=1328
\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\vec{b}=\lambda \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, \lambda \in \boldsymbol{Z}$ be two vectors. Let $\vec{c}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ and $\vec{d}$ be a vector of magnitude 2 in $y z$-plane. If $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=\sqrt{53}$, then the maximum possible value of $(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}})^{2}$ is equal to :
questionIds.solution:
$\vec{a} = 2\hat{i} - \hat{j} + \hat{k}$
$\vec{b} = \lambda\hat{j} + 2\hat{k}$ ; $\lambda \in Z$
$\vec{c} = \vec{a} \times \vec{b} = (-2 - \lambda)\hat{i} - 4\hat{j} + 2\lambda\hat{k}$
$|\vec{c}| = \sqrt{53}$
$\Rightarrow 5\lambda^2 + 4\lambda - 33 = 0$
$\lambda = 2.2$ or $-3$
$\Rightarrow \boxed{\lambda = -3}$
$\vec{c} = \hat{i} - 4\hat{j} - 6\hat{k}$
let $\vec{d} = y\hat{j} + z\hat{k}$
$|\vec{d}| = 2$
$\Rightarrow y^2 + z^2 = 4$
$(\vec{c} \cdot \vec{d})^2 = (4y + 6z)^2 \leq \left(\sqrt{4^2 + 6^2} \times \sqrt{y^2 + z^2}\right)^2 \leq 208$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
Let ann=0∞ be a sequence such that a0=a1=0 and an+2=3an+1-2an+1,∀n≥0.

Then a25a23-2a25a22-2a23a24+4a22a24 is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Given,

a0=0,a1=0 

And an+2=3aa+1-2an+1:n≥0

⇒an+2-an+1=2an+1-an+1

Now for n=0  a2-a1=2a1-a0+1 ....1

For n=1  a3-a2=2a2-a1+1 ........2

For n=2  a4-a3=2a3-a2+1 ........3

...

For n=n  an+2-an+1=2an+1-an+1 ......n

Now adding all above equation upto n we get,

an+2-a1-2an+1-a0-n+1=0

⇒an+2=2an+1+n+1

Now replacing n→n-2 we get,

⇒an-2an-1=n-1

Now putting n=25 we get,

⇒a25-2a24=25-1=24

Now putting n=23 we get,

⇒a23-2a22=23-1=22

So, a25-2a24a23-2a22=2422=528

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A river of width 200 m is flowing from west to east with a speed of $18 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. A boat, moving with speed of $36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ in still water, is made to travel one-round trip (bank to bank of the river). Minimum time taken by the boat for this journey and also the displacement along the river bank are $\_\_\_\_$ and $\_\_\_\_$ respectively.
questionIds.solution:
Width of the river $d = 200$ m.
Speed of river $v_r = 18$ km/h $= 18 \times \frac{5}{18} = 5$ m/s.
Speed of boat in still water $v_b = 36$ km/h $= 36 \times \frac{5}{18} = 10$ m/s.
For minimum time to cross the river, the boat must head perpendicular to the river flow (at $90^\circ$ to the bank).
Time taken to cross once $t = \frac{d}{v_b} = \frac{200}{10} = 20$ s.
For a round trip (bank to bank and back), the total minimum time is $T = 2t = 2 \times 20 = 40$ s.
During the first crossing, the drift (displacement along the bank) is $x_1 = v_r \times t = 5 \times 20 = 100$ m.
During the return trip, to minimize time, the boat again heads perpendicular to the flow relative to the water. The river continues to flow in the same direction, so the drift is again in the same direction as the first trip.
Return drift $x_2 = v_r \times t = 5 \times 20 = 100$ m.
Total displacement along the river bank $= x_1 + x_2 = 100 + 100 = 200$ m.
Thus, minimum time is $40$ s and displacement is $200$ m.

This galvanometer can be converted into (i) an ammeter of range 0 to <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> by connecting a shunt resistance <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow></msub></math> to it and (ii) into a voltmeter of range 0 to <math><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> by connecting a series resistance <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow></msub></math> to it. Then,
questionIds.solution:

For voltmeter: V0=IGG+RV
∵V0=I0×G
⇒IG×G+RV=IG×G+RA×GRA
⇒RA×G+RV×RA=G2+RA×G
⇒RVRA=G2
Again,   RARV=IG×GI0-IG×IGI0-IG×G=IG2I0-IG2

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The carrier frequency of a transmitter is provided by a tank circuit of a coil of inductance $49 \mu \mathrm{H}$ and a capactiance of $2.5 \mathrm{nF}$. It is modulated by an audio signal of $12 \mathrm{kHz}$. The frequency range occupied by the side bands is:
questionIds.solution:
Given: Inductance, $L=49 \mu \mathrm{H}=49 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{H}$, capacitance $C=2.5 \mathrm{nF}=2.5 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~F}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { Using } \omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}} \\
&=\frac{1}{\sqrt{49 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{2.5}{10} \times 10^{-9}}} \\
&=\frac{1}{7 \times 5 \times 10^{-8}}=\frac{10^8}{7 \times 5} \\
&\text { or, } \frac{10^8}{7 \times 5}=2 \pi \times f=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times f \\
&\text { or, } f=\frac{10^7}{22}=\frac{10^4}{22} \mathrm{kHz}=454.54 \mathrm{kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$
Therefore frequency range $454.54 \pm 12 \mathrm{kHz}$ i.e., $442 \mathrm{kHz}-466 \mathrm{kHz}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The effect of increase in temperature on the number of electrons in conduction band <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>n</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mo>)</mo></math> and resistance of a semiconductor will be as:
questionIds.solution: In case of semiconductors as the temperature increases the electrons in the valence band get excited and jump into the conduction band and hence the conductance increases resulting in the decrease of resistance. Thus, as temperature increases, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>n</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> increases and resistance decreases.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: If a wire is stretched to make it $0.1 \%$ longer, its resistance will :
questionIds.solution:
$\mathrm{R} \propto \ell^2$ (for a given volume)
$$
\Rightarrow \frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}} \%=\frac{2 \Delta \ell}{\ell} \%
$$
Thus when wire is stretched by $0.1 \%$ resistance increases by $0.2 \%$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: $5$ moles of unknown gas is heated at constant volume from $10 \, °C$ to $20 \, °C$. The molar specific heat of this gas at constant pressure $c_p = 8$ cal/mol.°C and $R = 8.36$ J/mol.°C. The change in the internal energy of the gas is _______ calorie.
questionIds.solution:
Given $n = 5$ moles, $\Delta T = 20^{\circ}\text{C} - 10^{\circ}\text{C} = 10^{\circ}\text{C}$.

The molar specific heat at constant pressure is $c_p = 8 \text{ cal/mol}\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}$.

The universal gas constant is $R = 8.36 \text{ J/mol}\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}$.

Converting $R$ into calories, we use $1 \text{ cal} = 4.18 \text{ J}$:

$R = \dfrac{8.36}{4.18} \text{ cal/mol}\cdot^{\circ}\text{C} = 2 \text{ cal/mol}\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}$

Using Mayer's relation, the molar specific heat at constant volume is:

$c_v = c_p - R = 8 - 2 = 6 \text{ cal/mol}\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}$

The change in internal energy of the gas is given by:

$\Delta U = n c_v \Delta T$

$\Delta U = 5 \times 6 \times 10 = 300 \text{ cal}$

Answer: $300$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Two bodies A and B of equal mass are suspended from two massless springs of spring constant $k_1$ and $k_2$, respectively. If the bodies oscillate vertically such that their amplitudes are equal, the ratio of the maximum velocity of $A$ to the maximum velocity of $B$ is
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}_1=\mathrm{A}_1 \omega_1 \\ & \mathrm{~V}_2=\mathrm{A}_2 \omega_2 \\ & \mathrm{~A}_1=\mathrm{A}_2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~V}_1}{\mathrm{~V}_2}=\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}=\frac{\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{K}_1}{\mathrm{~m}}}}{\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~K}_2}{\mathrm{~m}}}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~V}_1}{\mathrm{~V}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{K}_1}{\mathrm{~K}_2}}\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The velocity of a particle executing simple harmonic motion along $x$-axis is described as $v^2 = 50 - x^2$, where $x$ represents displacement. If the time period of motion is $\dfrac{x}{7}$ s, the value of $x$ is _____.
questionIds.solution:
The given equation is $v^2 = 50 - x^2$

Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$2v \dfrac{dv}{dx} = -2x$

$v \dfrac{dv}{dx} = -x$

Since acceleration $a = v \dfrac{dv}{dx}$, we get:

$a = -x$

Comparing this with the standard equation of SHM $a = -\omega^2 x$, we get:

$\omega^2 = 1 \Rightarrow \omega = 1$ rad/s

The time period of the motion is given by:

$T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega} = 2\pi$

Taking $\pi = \dfrac{22}{7}$, we get:

$T = 2 \times \dfrac{22}{7} = \dfrac{44}{7}$ s

Given that the time period is $\dfrac{x}{7}$ s, comparing the two gives:

$x = 44$

Answer: $44$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questionIds.q:
A pendulum of length 2 m consists of a wooden bob of mass 50 g. A bullet of mass 75 g is fired towards the stationary bob with a speed v. The bullet emerges out of the bob with a speed v3 and the bob just completes the vertical circle. The value of v is _____  m s-1

(if g=10 m s-2).
questionIds.solution:
For the bob to completer the vertical circle, vb=5gl.

From the conservation of momentum, 

m1v=m2vb+m1v3⇒75v=505gl+75v3

⇒752v3=505×10×2

⇒752v3=50×10

⇒v=500×3150=50050=10 m s-1

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: In a double slit experiment the distance between the slits is 0.1 cm and the screen is placed at 50 cm from the slits plane. When one slit is covered with a transparent sheet having thickness $t$ and refractive index $n(=1.5)$, the central fringe shifts by 0.2 cm. The value of $t$ is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
questionIds.solution:
Given: $d = 0.1$ cm, $D = 50$ cm, $n = 1.5$, Shift $= 0.2$ cm
Optical path difference by sheet: $\Delta = (n-1)t$
Shift of central fringe: Shift $= \frac{(n-1)t \times D}{d}$
$0.2 = \frac{(1.5-1) \times t \times 50}{0.1} = \frac{0.5 \times 50 \times t}{0.1} = 250t$
$t = \frac{0.2}{250} = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ cm

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
The current sensitivity of a galvanometer can be increased by :
(A) decreasing the number of turns
(B) increasing the magnetic field
(C) decreasing the area of the coil
(D) decreasing the torsional constant of the spring

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
questionIds.solution:
The current in the galvanometer is calculated by i=KNABθ.

Therefore, the sensitivity of the galvanometer,

dθdi=NABK

Hence, only options B and D are correct.

The loop is in a uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> whose direction is parallel to the current in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> side of the loop. The magnitude of the magnetic force on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> side will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>130</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______.
questionIds.solution:
Here, B=0.75 T

The magnitude of magnetic force on the 5 cm side is FAB=i×lABBsinθ           angle between AC & AB

=2×5100×0.75×1213

=9130 N

Hence, the value of x=9.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion (SHM). The ratio of potential energy and kinetic energy of the particle when its displacement is half of its amplitude will be
questionIds.solution:
The formula to calculate the potential energy of the particle executing SHM is given by

P=12mω2x2   ...1

The formula to calculate the kinetic energy of the same particle is given by

K=12mω2A2-x2   ...2

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) to obtain the required ratio.

PK=12mω2x212mω2A2-x2=x2A2-x2   ...3

Substitute A2 for x into equation (3) to calculate the required ratio.

PK=A22A2-A22= 13

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Substance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> has atomic mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math> and half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> day. Another substance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> has atomic mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>32</mn></math> and half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> day. If both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> simultaneously start undergo radio activity at the same time with initial mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>320</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> each, how many total atoms of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> combined would be left after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> days
questionIds.solution:
Number of nuclei left after n half-lives is given by,

N=N012tt12.

As, N0A=32016=20 moles and 

N0B=32032=10 moles

Then, NA=N0A221=204=5 and 

NB=N0B220.5=1024=0.625

So total N = 5.625

 and number of atoms =5.625×6.023×1023

=3.38×1024

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistor has certain colour code. If one replaced the red colour by green in the code, the new resistance will be:
questionIds.solution:
Colour band A and B indicate the first two significant figures of resistance in "ohm" , while the C band gives the decimal multiplier i.e., the number of zeros that follows the two significant figures A and B.

The last band D indicates the tolerance in percentage about the indicate value or in another ward it represents the percentage accuracy of the indicated value. 

 

As we know red corresponds to digit 2 and green corresponds to digit  5 , new resistance will be 500 Ω

=ΔTf'

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Given
ECl2/Cl-o=1.36V, ECr3+/Cro= -0.74V
ECr2O72-/Cr3+o=1.33V, EMnO4-/Mn2+o=1.51V.
Among the following, the strongest reducing agent is:
questionIds.solution: Strongest reducing agent has the highest tendency to get oxidised. Higher the oxidation potential, higher is the tendency to get oxidised. Reduction potential of Cr3+ is least (ECr3+/Cro= -0.74V) which indicates that oxidation potential of Cr is highest (ECr/Cr3+o= 0.74V). Thus, Cr is strongest reducing agent.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : The boiling points of alcohols and phenols increase with increase in the number of C-atoms.
Statement (II) : The boiling points of alcohols and phenols are higher in comparison to other class of compounds such as ethers, haloalkanes.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
questionIds.solution:
Statement I is correct as boiling point of alcohol phenols increase with increase in the number of C -atoms due to increase in van der Waals forces.
Statement II is correct, since alcohols phenols have intermolecular H -bonding therefore their boiling points are higher in comparison to other class of compounds such as ethers, haloalkanes.

Urea acts as a negative catalyst to this reaction and slow down the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Given below are two statements, one is labelled as

Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Beryllium has less negative value of reduction potential compared to the other alkaline earth metals

Reason R : Beryllium has large hydration energy due to small size of Be2+but relatively large value of atomisation enthalpy.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: A is correct but R is not correct
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
A is not correct but R is correct
Both A and R are correct and R is NOT the correct explanation of A
questionIds.solution: Beryllium has the smallest atomic size amongst the alkaline earth metals. The loss of electrons or oxidation is difficult. Beryllium has the least oxidation potential. As Beryllium has the least oxidation potential, it has the highest reduction potential(Less negative value of reduction potential). However, it's reducing nature is due to large hydration energy associated with the small size of Be2+ ion and relatively large value of the atomisation enthalpy of metal.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: The density (in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$ ) of a $3.60 \mathrm{~M}$ sulphuric acid solution that is $29 \% ~\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ (Molar mass $=98 \mathrm{~g}$ $\mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) by mass will be
questionIds.solution:
Let the density of solution be ' $\mathrm{d}$ '
Molarity of solution given $=3.6$
i.e. $1$ litre of solution contains $3.6$ moles of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$
or $1$ litre of solution contains $3.6 \times 98 \mathrm{~gms}$ of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$
Since, the solution is $29 \%$ by mass.
$100 \mathrm{~gm}$ solution contains $29 \mathrm{~gm} \mathrm{~H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ 
$\frac{100}{\mathrm{~d}} \mathrm{ml}$ solution contains $29 \mathrm{~gm}$ of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ 
$1000 \mathrm{~ml}$ solution contains $3.6 \times 98 \mathrm{~gm}$ of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ $\therefore 3.6 \times 98=\frac{29 \times \mathrm{d}}{100} \times 1000$ 
$d=1.22$
Hence, (C) is correct.

questionIds.q: At 600 K, the root mean square (rms) speed of gas X (molar mass =40) is equal to the most probable speed of gas Y at 90 K. The molar mass of the gas Y is _____ g mol–1. (Nearest integer)
questionIds.solution:
The root mean square velocity Vrms=3RTM

The most probable speed Vmp=2RTM

T=Temperature in Kelvin

M=Molar mass

Vrms of XVmp of Y=3TXMY2TYMX

1=3×600×MY2×90×40=MY4

MY=4 gm mol-1

questionIds.q:
Ethylene glycol is used as an antifreeze in a cold climate. Mass of ethylene glycol which should be added to $4 \mathrm{~kg}$ of water to prevent it from freezing at $-6^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ will be :
$\left[\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{t}}\right.$ for water $=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, and molar mass of ethylene glycol $=62 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
questionIds.solution:
$$
\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \mathrm{m}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \frac{\mathrm{w}_2 \times 1000}{\mathrm{w}_1 \times \mathrm{m}_2}
$$
$w_1 \& w_2=$ wt of solvent \& solute respecting $m_2=m w$ of solute
$$
\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=0^{\circ}-\left(-6^{\circ}\right)=6=1.86 \times \frac{\mathrm{w}_2 \times 1000}{4000 \times 62}
$$
Therefore $w_2=800 \mathrm{~g}$
