questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
The rate of a reaction quadruples when the temperature changes from 300  to 310 K . The activation energy of this reaction is:

(Assume Activation energy and pre-exponential factor are independent of temperature; ln(2)=0.693; R=8.314 J mol-1K-1)
questionIds.solution:
The activation energy, rate constant and temperature can be related using Arrhenius equation.

k=Ae-EaRT

lnk2k1=EaR 1T1-1T2 

ln4=EaR 1300-1310 

2×0.693=EaR 10300×310 

E a  =  2×0.693×300×310×8.314 10  = 107.2 kJ/mol

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>tan</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>sec</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> has exactly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> solutions in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>, for the least value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></munderover><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></msup></mfrac></math> is equal to :
questionIds.solution:
Given,

2tan2θ-5secθ-1=0

⇒2sec2θ-1-5secθ-1=0

⇒2sec2θ-5secθ-3=0

⇒2sec2θ-6secθ+secθ-3=0

⇒2secθsecθ-3+1secθ-3=0

⇒(2secθ+1)(secθ-3)=0

⇒secθ=-12,3

⇒cosθ=-2, 13

We know that, -1≤cosθ≤1

⇒cosθ=13

For 7 solutions n=13

So, ∑k=113k2k=S (say)

⇒S=12+222+323+….+13213

⇒12 S=122+123+…..+12213+13214

⇒S-S2=12+222+323+….+13213-122+123+…..+12213+13214

⇒S2=12+122+323+...+13213-13214

⇒S2=12·1-12131-12-13214

⇒S2=12·213-121312-13214

⇒S=2·213-1213-13213

⇒S=214-2-13213

⇒S=214-15213

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let $\cos (\alpha+\beta)=-\frac{1}{10}$ and $\sin (\alpha-\beta)=\frac{3}{8}$, where $0<\alpha<\frac{\pi}{3}$ and $0<\beta<\frac{\pi}{4}$. If $\tan 2 \alpha=\frac{3(1-r \sqrt{5})}{\sqrt{11}(s+\sqrt{5})}, r, s \in N$, then $r+s$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questionIds.solution:
$\tan 2 \alpha=\tan [(\alpha+\beta)+(\alpha-\beta)]$
$\tan 2 \alpha=\frac{\tan (\alpha+\beta)+\tan (\alpha-\beta)}{1-\tan (\alpha+\beta) \cdot \tan (\alpha-\beta)}$
$\tan 2 \alpha=\frac{\left(-\sqrt{99}+\frac{3}{\sqrt{55}}\right)}{1-(\sqrt{99})\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{55}}\right)}$
$\tan 2 \alpha=\frac{-3 \sqrt{11}+\frac{3}{\sqrt{5} \times \sqrt{11}}}{1+\frac{9 \sqrt{11}}{\sqrt{5} \times \sqrt{11}}}$
$\tan 2 \alpha=\frac{3(1-11 \sqrt{5})}{\sqrt{11}(9+\sqrt{5})}$
$\mathrm{r}=11, \mathrm{~s}=9$
$\mathrm{r}+\mathrm{s}=20$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></math> are the roots of the equation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2023</mn><msup><mi>α</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2024</mn><msup><mi>β</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msup></math>, then
questionIds.solution:
Given: α, β are roots of x2-x-1=0.

⇒α2-α-1=0

⇒α2=α+1   ...i

Also, Sn=2023αn+2024βn

⇒Sn-1+Sn-2=2023αn-1+2024βn-1+2023αn-2+2024βn-2

⇒Sn-1+Sn-2=2023αn-21+α+2024βn-21+β

Using equation i,

⇒Sn-1+Sn-2=2023αn-2α2+2024βn-2β2

⇒Sn-1+Sn-2=2023αn+2024βn

⇒Sn-1+Sn-2=Sn

Putting, n=12

⇒S12=S11+S10

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If $f$ is a real-valued differentiable function satisfying $|f(x)-f(y)| \leq(x-y)^2, x, y \in R$ and $f(0)=0$, then $f(1)$ equals
questionIds.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& f^{\prime}(x)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h} \\
& \left|f^{\prime}(x)\right|=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left|\frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}\right| \leq \lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left|\frac{(h)^2}{h}\right| \\
& \Rightarrow\left|f^{\prime}(x)\right| \leq 0 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=0 \quad \Rightarrow f(x)=\text { constant } \\
& \text { As } f(0)=0 \Rightarrow f(1)=0 .
\end{aligned}
$$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If $|\vec{c}|^2=60$ and $\vec{c} \times(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})=\overrightarrow{0}$, then a value of $\vec{c} \cdot(-7 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})$ is:
questionIds.solution:
Let, $\vec{c}=a \hat{i}+b \hat{j}+c \hat{k}$
Given, $\vec{c} \times(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})=\overrightarrow{0}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\
a & b & c \\
1 & 2 & 5
\end{array}\right|=\overrightarrow{0} \\
&\Rightarrow(5 b-2 c) \hat{i}-(5 a-c) \hat{j}+(2 a-b) \hat{k} \\
&=0 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+0 \hat{k}
\end{aligned}
$$
Comparing both sides, we get $5 b-2 c=0 ; 5 a-c=0 ; 2 a-b=0$ or $5 b=2 c ; 5 a=c ; 2 a=b$
Also given $|\vec{c}|^2=60$
$$
\Rightarrow a^2+b^2+c^2=60
$$
Putting the value of $b$ and $c$ in above eqn., we get

$$
\begin{aligned}
&a^2+(2 a)^2+(5 a)^2=60 \\
&\Rightarrow a^2+4 a^2+25 a^2=60 \Rightarrow 30 a^2=60 \\
&a^2=2 \\
&a=\pm \sqrt{2} ; b=2 \sqrt{2} ; c=5 \sqrt{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, $\vec{c}=a \hat{i}+b \hat{j}+c \hat{k}$
$$
\therefore \vec{c}=\sqrt{2} \hat{i}+2 \sqrt{2} \hat{j}+5 \sqrt{2} \hat{k}
$$
Value of $\vec{c} \cdot(-7 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})$ is
$$
\begin{aligned}
&(\sqrt{2} \hat{i}+2 \sqrt{2} \hat{j}+5 \sqrt{2} \hat{k}) \cdot(-7 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \\
&=-7 \sqrt{2}+4 \sqrt{2}+15 \sqrt{2}=12 \sqrt{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let the Mean and Variance of five observations $x_1=1, x_2=3, x_3=a, x_4=7$ and $x_5=b, a \gt b$, be 5 and 10 respectively. Then the Variance of the observations $n+x_n, n=1,2, \ldots \ldots . .5$ is
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \bar{x}=\frac{\sum x_i}{n}=\frac{1+3+a+7+b}{5}=5 \\ & a+b=14 \\ & \sigma^2=\frac{\sum x_i^2}{n}-(\bar{x})^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1^2+3^2+a^2+7^2+b^2}{5}-25=10 \\ & a^2+b^2=116 \\ & a \gt b \quad a=10 \quad b=4 \\ & n+x_n: 2,5,13,11,9\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \sigma^2=\frac{2^2+5^2+13^2+11^2+9^2}{5}-\left(\frac{2+5+13+11+9}{5}\right)^2 \\ & =80-64=16\end{aligned}$
option 4

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Consider a rectangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi></math> having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>9</mn></math> points in the interior of the line segments <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>D</mi><mi>A</mi></math> respectively. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> be the number of triangles having these points from different sides as vertices and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> be the number of quadrilaterals having these points from different sides as vertices. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>β</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
α= Number of triangles

α=5·6·7+5·7·9+5·6·9+6·7·9

=210+315+270+378

=1173

β= Number of Quadrilateral

β=5·6·7·9=1890

β-α=1890-1173=717

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>λ</mi><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mi>λ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℝ</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is a vector such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>×</mo><mover accent="true"><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>13</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>·</mo><mover accent="true"><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>21</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mover accent="true"><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover accent="true"><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>·</mo><mfenced><mrow><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mover accent="true"><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>·</mo><mfenced><mrow><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Let a→=xi^+yj^+zk^

So,i^j^k^xyz11λ=i^λy-z+j^z-λx+k^x-y

On comparing with 13i^-j^-4k^ we get,

⇒λy-z=13,z-λx=-1,x-y=-4

and x+y+λz=-21

⇒ Clearly, λ=3,x=-2,y=2 and z=-7

So, b→-a→=3i^-j^+10k^ and b→+a→=-i^+3j^-4k^

⇒b→-a→·k^-j^+b→+a→·i^-k^=11+3

=14

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>C</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>−</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>λ</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> be two circles. If the set of all values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> so that the circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>'</mo></math> intersect at two distinct points, is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>−</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>8</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>16</mn><mi>b</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>20</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> lies on the curve:
questionIds.solution:
Given: x2+y2=4

So, centre and radius of C are 0,0 and r1=2 respectively.

Also, C':x2+y2−4λx+9=0

So, centre and radius of C' are 2λ,0 and r2=4λ2−9 respectively.

⇒r1−r2<CC'<r1+r2

⇒2−4λ2−9<2λ<2+4λ2−9

⇒4+4λ2−9−44λ2−9<4λ2

⇒λ∈R   ...i

Also, 4λ2<4+4λ2−9+44λ2−9

⇒5<44λ2−9 λ2≥94⇒λ∈−∞,−32∪32,∞

⇒2516<4λ2−9

⇒16964<λ2

⇒16964<λ2

⇒λ∈−∞,−138∪138,∞   ...ii

Using i and ii,

⇒λ∈−∞,−138∪138,∞

⇒λ∈R−−138,138

As per question a=−138 and b=138

∴ required point is 8a+12, 16b-20≡-1, 6 with satisfies option (d).

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let an ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>:</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>></mo><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, passes through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msqrt><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and has eccentricity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math>. If a circle, centered at focus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>, of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math>, intersects <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> at two points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><msup><mi>Q</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to :
questionIds.solution:
The ellipse x2a2+y2b2=1 passes through the point 32,1

So, 32a2+1b2=1   ...1 and 1-b2a2=13   ...2

On solving equations 1 and 2, we get

⇒a2=3 & b2=2

⇒x23+y22=1   ...3

We know that, focii of the ellipse x2a2+y2b2=1 is ±ae, 0

Here, a=3 & e=13

∴ Its focus is 1,0 ∵α>0

Now, equation of circle is

(x-1)2+y2=43   ...4

Solving 3 and 4 we get

y=±23,x=1

⇒PQ2=1-12+23+232

=432=163

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Consider the integral I=∫010xexex-1dx where x denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to x. Then the value of I is equal to :
questionIds.solution:
I=∫010x·ex-x+1

I=∫010dx+∫121·e2-x+∫232·e3-x+…..+∫9109·e10-xdx

⇒I=∑n=09∫nn+1n·en+1-xdx

=-∑n=09nen+1-xnn+1

=-∑n=09n·e0-e1

=e-1∑n=09n

=e-1·9·102

=45e-1

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If three distinct numbers <math><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi></math> are in G.P. and the equations <math><mi>a</mi><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math><mi>d</mi><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>e</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>f</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> have a common root, then which one of the following statements is correct?
questionIds.solution:
∵ a, b, c are in G.P. ⇒b2=ac …(i)
Equation ax2+2bx+c=0
Hence, D=4b2-4ac=0 ∵b2=ac
⇒ equation has equal roots ⇒ roots are =-ba, -ba ( ∵ sum of roots α+α=-2ba )
∵ Both equations have one root in common.
∴ Common root is -ba. It will satisfy equation.
∴d-ba2+2e-ba+f=0⇒db2-2aeb+a2f=0
⇒dac-2aeb+a2f=0 ∵b2=ac
⇒dc-2aeb+af=0
⇒dcac-2ebac+afac=0 (dividing by ac )
⇒da-2ebb2+fc=-⇒da+fc=2eb
⇒da,eb,fc are in A.P.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If the equation of the hyperbola with foci $(4,2)$ and $(8,2)$ is $3 x^2-y^2-\alpha x+\beta y+\gamma=0$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma$ is equal to _____.
questionIds.solution:
Equation of hyperbola is
$\frac{(x-6)^2}{a^2}-\frac{(y-2)^2}{4-a^2}=1$
$\Rightarrow\left(4-\mathrm{a}^2\right)(\mathrm{x}-6)^2-\mathrm{a}^2(\mathrm{y}-2)^2=\mathrm{a}^2\left(4-\mathrm{a}^2\right)$
comparing with $3 x^2-y^2-\alpha x+\beta y+\gamma=0$, we get $\mathrm{a}^2=1$ and $\alpha=36, \beta=4$ and $\gamma=101$
$\therefore \alpha+\beta+\gamma=141$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Which of the following statements is a tautology?
questionIds.solution:
Plotting the truth table of the following option we have,

p q ~p ~q ~p∨q
T T F F T
T F F T F
F T T F T
F F T T T

We know that, A⇒B is only false when A is true and B is false, now using this rule and above table we will make truth table of options,

~p∨q⇒p p⇒~p∨q ~p∨q⇒q q⇒~p∨q
T T T T
T F T T
F T T T
F T F T

 

Hence q⇒~p∨q is tautology.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: A spherical iron ball of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> is coated with a layer of ice of uniform thickness that melts at a rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>/</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When the thickness of the ice is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the rate at which the thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo></math>in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>)</mo></math> of the ice decreases, is :
questionIds.solution:
We know that the volume of a sphere of radius r is 43πr3.

Given the radius of the spherical iron ball is R=10 cm and the thickness of the ice is x cm.


Volume of ice: V=43πR+x3-43πR3

⇒V=43π10+x3-43π103

Differentiating with respect to t, we get

dVdt=4π10+x2dxdt-0

Given dVdt=50 cm3/min

⇒50=4π10+x2dxdt

⇒dxdt=504π10+x2

⇒dxdtx=5=504π152=118π cm/min.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If B is a 3×3  matrix such that B2=0, then det. I+B50-50B is equal to :
questionIds.solution:
B2=0⇒B4=B6=B8=....=B50=0  and B3=B2B=0B=0

⇒ I+B50=I+C150B (higher powers are zero)

∴ det.I+B50-50B 

  =det. I+50C1B-50B

  =det.I

  =1

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>A</mi><mn>2025</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>A</mi><mn>2020</mn></msup></math> is equal to

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Two equal sides of an isosceles triangle are along $-x+2 y=4$ and $x+y=4$. If m is the slope of its third side, then the sum, of all possible distinct values of $m$, is :
questionIds.solution: Slope of the third side = slope of the perpendicular bisector of given lines $h: \frac{-x+2 y-4}{\sqrt{5}}= \pm \frac{x+y-4}{\sqrt{2}}$ $\begin{aligned} & h_1: \sqrt{2}(-x+2 y-4)=\sqrt{5}(x+y-4) \\ & h_2: \sqrt{2}(-x+2 y-4)=-\sqrt{5}(x+y-4) \\ & M_{L_1}:-\left[\frac{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}-2 \sqrt{2}}\right] \\ & M_{L_2}:-\left[\frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}+2 \sqrt{2}}\right] \\ & M_{L_1}+M_{L_2}=-\left[\frac{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}-2 \sqrt{2}}+\frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}+2 \sqrt{2}}\right]\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & =-\left[\frac{(\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2})(\sqrt{5}+2 \sqrt{2})+(\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2})(\sqrt{5}-2 \sqrt{2})}{-3}\right] \\ & =6\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The locus of the vertices of the family of parabolas $y=\frac{a^3 x^2}{3}+\frac{a^2 x}{2}-2 a$ is
questionIds.solution:
Parabola: $y=\frac{a^3 x^2}{3}+\frac{a^2 x}{2}-2 a$
Vertex: $(\alpha, \beta)$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \alpha=\frac{-a^2 / 2}{2 a^3 / 3}=-\frac{3}{4 a}, \beta=\frac{-\left(\frac{a^4}{4}+4 \cdot \frac{a^3}{3} \cdot 2 a\right)}{4 \frac{a^3}{3}}=-\frac{-\left(\frac{1}{4}+\frac{8}{3}\right) a^4}{\frac{4}{3} a^3} \\
& =-\frac{35}{12} \frac{a}{4} \times 3=-\frac{35}{16} a \\
& \alpha \beta=-\frac{3}{4 a}\left(-\frac{35}{16}\right) a=\frac{105}{64} .
\end{aligned}
$$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The values of ' $a$ ' for which one root of the equation $x^2-(a+1) x+a^2+a-8=0$ exceeds 2 and the other is lesser than 2 , are given by :
questionIds.solution:
$$
x^2-(a+1) x+a^2+a-8=0
$$
Since roots are different, therefore $\mathrm{D}>0$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow(a+1)^2-4\left(a^2+a-8\right)>0 \\
& \Rightarrow(a-3)(3 a+1) < 0
\end{aligned}
$$
There are two cases arises.
Case I. $a-3>0$ and $3 a+1 < 0$
$\Rightarrow a>3$ and $a < -\frac{11}{3}$
Hence, no solution in this case
Case II : $a-3 < 0$ and $3 a+11>0$
$\Rightarrow a < 3$ and $a>-\frac{11}{3}$
$\therefore \quad-\frac{11}{3} < a < 3 \Rightarrow-2 < a < 3$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The number of complex numbers $z$ such that $|z-1|=|z+1|=|z-i|$ equals
questionIds.solution:
Let $z=x+i y$
$$
\begin{array}{lll}
|z-1|=|z+1| & \Rightarrow \operatorname{Re} z=0 & \Rightarrow x=0 \\
|z-1|=|z-i| & \Rightarrow x=y & \\
|z+1|=|z-i| & \Rightarrow y=-x &
\end{array}
$$
Only $(0,0)$ will satisfy all conditions.
$\Rightarrow$ Number of complex number $z=1$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The sum of all two digit positive numbers which when divided by <math><mn>7</mn></math> yield <math><mn>2</mn></math> or <math><mn>5</mn></math> as remainder is
questionIds.solution:
Two-digit numbers of the form 7λ+2 are 16, 23, 30,..., 93

These number are forming an arithmetic progression with first term a=16, common difference d=23-16=7 and last term l=93

And, we know that the last term of an arithmetic progression is given by l=a+n-1d

⇒93=16+n-17

⇒77=n-17

⇒n=12

Two-digit numbers of the form 7λ+5 are 12, 19, 26,..., 96

Again, using the last term of A.P., we get 96=12+n1-17

⇒84=n1-17

⇒n1=13

Now, the sum of n terms of an A.P. is Sn=n2a+l, we get the sum of all the above numbers as

=12216+93+13212+96

=654+702=1356.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let $P$ be a point in the plane of the vectors $\overrightarrow{A B}=3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{A C}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ such that $P$ is equidistant from the lines AB and AC. If $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AP}}|=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}$, then the area of the triangle ABP is:
questionIds.solution:
Since P is equidistant from lines AB and AC through point A, it lies along the angle bisector direction. The unit vectors are $\hat{u}_{AB} = \frac{3\vec{i} + \vec{j} - \vec{k}}{\sqrt{11}}$ and $\hat{u}_{AC} = \frac{\vec{i} - \vec{j} + 3\vec{k}}{\sqrt{11}}$.
The bisector direction is proportional to $2\vec{i} + \vec{k}$, so $\vec{AP} = \lambda(2\vec{i} + \vec{k})$.
From $|\vec{AP}| = \frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}$: $|\lambda|\sqrt{5} = \frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}$, giving $\lambda = \frac{1}{2}$.
Thus $\vec{AP} = \vec{i} + \frac{1}{2}\vec{k}$.
Area of triangle ABP = $\frac{1}{2}|\vec{AB} \times \vec{AP}|$.
Computing: $\vec{AB} \times \vec{AP} = \frac{1}{2}\vec{i} - \frac{5}{2}\vec{j} - \vec{k}$.
$|\vec{AB} \times \vec{AP}| = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4} + \frac{25}{4} + 1} = \sqrt{\frac{30}{4}} = \frac{\sqrt{30}}{2}$.
Area = $\frac{\sqrt{30}}{4}$.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The negation of the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>↔</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>y</mi></math> is equivalent to:
questionIds.solution:
Negation of x↔~y

≡~x↔~y

≡~x→~y∧~y→x

≡~x→~y∨~~y→x (using De'Morgan's laws)

≡x∧y∨~y∧~x

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>x</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>, </mo><mover><mi>y</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>z</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>, then the magnitude of the projection of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>x</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>×</mo><mover><mi>y</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>z</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is
questionIds.solution:
Given x→=3i^-6j^-k^, y→=i^+4j^-3k^ and z→=3i^-4j^-12k^.

⇒x→×y→=i^j^k^3-6-114-3

=i^18+4-j^-9+1+k^12+6=22i^+8j^+18k^=211i^+4j^+9k^

Now, we know that the projection of a vector a→ on b→ is defined as a→·b→b→

Hence, the magnitude of projection of x→×y→ on z→ is x→×y→·z→z→

=211i+4j+9k·3i^-4j^-12k^3i^-4j^-12k^

=233-16-10832+42+122

=2×91169=2×9113=14.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If limx→∞x2-x+1-ax=b, then the ordered pair (a, b) is:
questionIds.solution:
Given that:limx→∞ x2−x+1−ax=b

⇒limx→∞ x2−x+1−ax×x2−x+1+axx2−x+1+ax=b

⇒limx→∞ x2−x+1−(ax)2x2−x+1+ax=b

⇒ Limit exist only If a2=1, because x2 must not be there in the numerator 

∴a=1,−1

⇒limx→∞ −x+1x2−x+1+ax=b

⇒limx→∞ −1+1x1−1x+1x2+a=b

⇒−11+a=b

Here, a≠−1

So a=1

b=−12

(a,b)≡1,−12

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The integral <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mn>6</mn></mfrac><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msubsup><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></mrow><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>·</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msup><mi>x</mi><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Let I=∫π6π3sec23x·cosec43xdx

⇒I=∫π6π31cos23x·sin43xdx

⇒I=∫π6π3cos43xcos23+43x·sin43xdx

⇒I=∫π6π3sec2xtan43xdx

Let tanx=t, sec2xdx=dt and at x=π6, t=tanπ6=13 and at x=π3, t=tanπ3=3

⇒I=∫133dtt43

Using ∫xndx=xn+1n+1

⇒I=t-43+1-43+1133

⇒I=-3t-13133

⇒I=-33-13-13-13

⇒I=-3312-13-1312-13

=-31316-316

=-3316+3×316

=376-356.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If the circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>16</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfenced><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo><msqrt><mn>6</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfenced><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><msqrt><mn>6</mn></msqrt></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>, touch internally at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>6</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to _______.
questionIds.solution:
The circle x2+y2+6x+8y+16=0 has centre -3,-4 and radius 9+16-16=3 units.

The circle x2+y2+23-3x+24-6y= k+63+86,k>0 has centre 3-3,6-4 and radius 3-32+6-42+k+63+86=k+34

Given that these two circles touch internally, so

distance between their centres=difference of radii

3+6=k+34-3

⇒k+34-3=±3

Here, k=2 is only possible value  ∵  k>0

Now the equation of common tangent to both the circles is given by 23x+26y+16+k+63+86=0

∵  k=2 then equation becomes 

x+2y+33+3+42=0     ⋯i

∵  α,β are foot of perpendicular from -3,-4 to this common tangent, then

α+31=β+42=--3-42+3+42+331+2

∴ α+3=-3 & β+42=-3

⇒α+32=9 and β+62=16

Hence, α+32+β+62=25

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
The values of $m, n$, for which the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& x+y+z=4, \\
& 2 x+5 y+5 z=17, \\
& x+2 y+\mathrm{m} z=\mathrm{n}
\end{aligned}$
has infinitely many solutions, satisfy the equation:
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{D}=\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & 5 \\ 1 & 2 & \mathrm{~m}\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=2 \\ & \mathrm{D}_3=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 4 \\ 2 & 5 & 17 \\ 1 & 2 & \mathrm{n}\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=7\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A circular current loop of radius $R$ is placed inside square loop of side length $L$ $(L >> R)$ such that they are co-planar and their centers coincide. The permeability of free space is $\mu_0$. The mutual inductance between circular loop and square loop is _______.
questionIds.solution:
Using the reciprocity theorem, assume a current $I$ flows through the larger square loop and compute the flux through the smaller circular loop. The mutual inductance is given by $M = \dfrac{\Phi}{I}$.

Magnetic field at the centre of the square loop:
For a square of side $L$, the perpendicular distance from the centre to any side is $d = \dfrac{L}{2}$, and each side subtends angles $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 45^\circ$ at the centre.

The field due to one finite straight segment is:
$B_{\text{segment}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi d}(\sin\theta_1 + \sin\theta_2)$

$B_{\text{segment}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi \cdot \frac{L}{2}}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi L} \cdot \sqrt{2} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 I}{2\pi L}$

All four sides contribute equally in the same direction at the centre, so:
$B_{\text{total}} = 4 \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 I}{2\pi L} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 I}{\pi L}$

Flux through the circular loop:
Since $L \gg R$, the field $B_{\text{total}}$ can be treated as uniform over the area $A = \pi R^2$ of the circular loop.

$\Phi = B_{\text{total}} \cdot A = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 I}{\pi L} \cdot \pi R^2 = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 R^2 I}{L}$

Mutual inductance:
$M = \dfrac{\Phi}{I} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 R^2}{L}$

Hence, the correct option is $(4)\ \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 R^2}{L}$.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A uniform metal chain of mass m and length L passes over a massless and frictionless pulley. It is released from rest with a part of its length l is hanging on one side and rest of its length L-l  is hanging on the other side of the pulley. At a certain point of time, when l=Lx, the acceleration of the chain is g2. The value of x is _____.
questionIds.solution:
According to the information given in the question, the part of the chain that envelopes the pulley is very small. Therefore, the acceleration of the chain can be written as,

a=m2-m1m2+m1g

Since a=g2

g2=m2-m1m2+m1g⇒g2=L-l-lLg⇒l=L4=Lx

⇒x=4

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The displacement of an object attached to a spring and executing simple harmonic motion is given by $x=2 \times 10^{-2} \cos \pi t$ metres. The time at which the maximum speed first occurs is
questionIds.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{x}=2 \times 10^{-2} \cos \pi t \\
& \mathrm{v}=-0.02 \pi \sin \pi t
\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{v}$ is maximum at $\mathrm{t}=\frac{1}{2}=0.5 \mathrm{~sec}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A convex lens is made from glass material having refractive index of $1.4$ with same radius of curvature on both sides. The ratio of its focal length and radius of curvature is ______.
questionIds.solution:
Using the lens maker's formula:

$\dfrac{1}{f} = (\mu - 1) \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2} \right)$

For an equiconvex lens, $R_1 = R$ and $R_2 = -R$. Given $\mu = 1.4$:

$\dfrac{1}{f} = (1.4 - 1) \left( \dfrac{1}{R} - \dfrac{1}{-R} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{f} = 0.4 \times \dfrac{2}{R}$

$\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{0.8}{R}$

$\dfrac{f}{R} = \dfrac{1}{0.8} = 1.25$

Answer: $1.25$

questionIds.q: Two bodies of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> are moving with equal kinetic energies. K=12mv2=p22m   ...1

Following equation (1), for the two given bodies, it can be written that

p122 m1=p222 m2⇒p1p2=m1 m2=425=25

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: An unpolarised light is incident at an interface of two dielectric media having refractive indices of 2 (incident medium) and $2 \sqrt{3}$ (medium) respectively. To satisfy the condition that reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular to each other, the angle of incidence is $\_\_\_\_$.
questionIds.solution:
For reflected and refracted rays to be perpendicular, if $i$ is the angle of incidence and $r$ is the angle of refraction, then $i + r = 90°$, giving $r = 90° - i$.
Applying Snell's law: $n_1 \sin i = n_2 \sin r$
$2 \sin i = 2\sqrt{3} \sin(90° - i)$
$2 \sin i = 2\sqrt{3} \cos i$
$\tan i = \sqrt{3}$
$i = 60°$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The height of transmitting antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the height of the receiving antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>245</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight will be: (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
questionIds.solution:
The maximum distance for line of sight communication is 

d=2hR+2h'R

The given data is 

R=6400 kmh=180 m

h'=245 m

Thus, the value is

d=2×180×6400×103

+2×245×6400×103

=6×80×102+7×80×102

=104 km

After disintegration of 78thpart, 18th part is only left.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Two closed vessels of same volume are joined through a narrow tube and both vessels are filled with air of pressure $90$ kPa and temperature $400$ K. Keeping the temperature of one vessel constant at $400$ K the second vessel temperature is raised to $500$ K. The final pressure in the vessels is _______ kPa.
questionIds.solution:
Let the volume of each vessel be $V$.

Initially, both vessels are at pressure $P_1 = 90$ kPa and temperature $T_1 = 400$ K.

Total number of moles initially is given by:

$n_i = \dfrac{P_1 V}{R T_1} + \dfrac{P_1 V}{R T_1} = \dfrac{2 P_1 V}{R T_1}$

Finally, the temperature of the first vessel is $T_1 = 400$ K and the second vessel is $T_2 = 500$ K. Let the final pressure be $P_f$.

Total number of moles finally is given by:

$n_f = \dfrac{P_f V}{R T_1} + \dfrac{P_f V}{R T_2}$

Since the total number of moles remains constant, $n_i = n_f$:

$\dfrac{2 P_1 V}{R T_1} = \dfrac{P_f V}{R T_1} + \dfrac{P_f V}{R T_2}$

Substituting the given values:

$\dfrac{2 \times 90}{400} = \dfrac{P_f}{400} + \dfrac{P_f}{500}$

$\dfrac{180}{400} = P_f \left( \dfrac{1}{400} + \dfrac{1}{500} \right)$

$\dfrac{180}{400} = P_f \left( \dfrac{5 + 4}{2000} \right)$

$\dfrac{180}{400} = P_f \left( \dfrac{9}{2000} \right)$

$P_f = \dfrac{180 \times 2000}{400 \times 9}$

$P_f = 20 \times 5 = 100$ kPa

Answer: $100$

questionIds.q: For <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><msup><mi>y</mi><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></msup></math>, where '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>' is a constant. If percentage error in measurement of  '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>' and '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>' are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>%</mo></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>%</mo></math>, respectively, then the percentage error for '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math>' will be _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math>.
questionIds.solution:
Given: z=a2x3y12

Relative error is given by 

∆zz=3×∆xx+12×∆yy

Now, maximum percentage error in z is 

∆zz×100=34+122×100

=18%.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}_1=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{R}_1}=\frac{12}{2}=6 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{dl_{2 }}}{\mathrm{dt}}+\mathrm{R}_2 \times \mathrm{I}_2 \\ & \mathrm{I}_2=\mathrm{I}_0\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t} / \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{c}}}\right) \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{o}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}_2}=\frac{12}{2}=6 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{400 \times 10^{-3}}{2}=0.2 \\ & \mathrm{I}_2=6\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t} / 0.2}\right) \end{aligned} $$ Potential drop across $L=E-R_2 l_2=12-2 \times 6\left(1-e^{-b t}\right)=12 e^{-5 t}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A spherical ball of mass $2$ kg falls from a height of $10$ m and is brought to rest after penetrating $10$ cm into sand. The average force exerted by sand on the ball is _______ N. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
questionIds.solution:
Using the work-energy theorem, the total work done on the ball is equal to its change in kinetic energy.

Since the ball starts from rest and finally comes to rest, the change in kinetic energy is zero.

$W_{\text{gravity}} + W_{\text{sand}} = \Delta K = 0$

The work done by gravity is over the total distance $h + d$, and the work done by the resistive force of the sand $F$ is over the distance $d$.

$mg(h + d) - Fd = 0$

$F = \dfrac{mg(h + d)}{d}$

Given $m = 2$ kg, $g = 10$ m/s$^2$, $h = 10$ m, and $d = 10$ cm $= 0.1$ m:

$F = \dfrac{2 \times 10 \times (10 + 0.1)}{0.1}$

$F = \dfrac{20 \times 10.1}{0.1}$

$F = 20 \times 101 = 2020$ N

Answer: $2020$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A capacitor of reactance $4 \sqrt{3} \Omega$ and a resistor of resistance $4 \Omega$ are connected in series with an ac source of peak value $8 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~V}$. The power dissipation in the circuit is ________ W.
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{X}^2 \mathrm{~L}} \\ & \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{4^2+(4 \sqrt{3})^2}=8 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{8 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}}=(8 \mathrm{~V}) \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}}{\mathrm{Z}}=\frac{8}{8}=1 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$ Power dissipated $=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}^2 \times \mathrm{R}=1 \times 4=(4 \mathrm{~W})$

questionIds.q: Monochromatic light is incident on a glass prism of angle A. If the refractive index of the material of the prism is μ , a ray, incident at an angle θ , on the face AB would get transmitted through the face AC of the prism provided:
options: <math> <mrow> <mi>θ</mi> <mo><</mo> <msup> <mtext>cos</mtext> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mfenced close="]" open="[" separators=","> <mrow> <mi>μ</mi> <mo>sin</mo> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mtext>A</mtext> <mo>+</mo> <msup> <mtext>sin</mtext> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mi>μ</mi> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math>

<math> <mrow> <mi>θ</mi> <mo><</mo> <msup> <mtext>sin</mtext> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mfenced close="]" open="[" separators=","> <mrow> <mi>μ</mi> <mo>sin</mo> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mtext>A</mtext> <mo>-</mo> <msup> <mtext>sin</mtext> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mi>μ</mi> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math>
questionIds.solution:
Central Idea: The ray will get transmitted through face AC if iAC<iC

Consider the ray diagram is shown below.

A ray of light incident on face AB at an angle θ

r1= Angle of refraction on face AB

r2= Angle of incidence at face AC

For transmission of light through face AC

iAC<iC

or A-r1<iC

or sinA-r1<siniC

or sinA-r1<1μ

A-r1<sin-11μ

or sinr1>sinA-sin-11μ

Now, applying Snell's law at the face AB

1×sinθ=μsinr1

or sinr1=sinθμ

⇒sinθμ>sinA-sin-11μ

or θ>sin-1μsinA-sin-11μ

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: In Bohr's atomic model of hydrogen, let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> are the kinetic energy, potential energy and total energy of the electron respectively. Choose the correct option when the electron undergoes transitions to a higher level :
questionIds.solution:
Total energy of electron is stated as E=-Z2me48nhε02, potential energy P=-Z2me44nhε02 and kinetic energy is given by K=Z2me48nhε02.

So, as electron makes transition to higher level, total energy and potential energy increases due to the negative sign while the kinetic energy of electron decreases.

questionIds.q: A capacitor, $C_1=6 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ is charged to a potential difference of $\mathrm{V}_0=5 \mathrm{~V}$ using a 5 V battery. The battery is removed and another capacitor, $\mathrm{C}_2=12 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ is inserted in place of the battery. When the switch 'S' is closed, the charge flows between the capacitors for some time until equilibrium condition is reached. What are the charges $\left(q_1\right.$ and $\left.q_2\right)$ on the capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ when equilibrium condition is reached.
questionIds.solution:
$\mathrm{q}_1^{\prime}=6 \times 5=30 \mu \mathrm{C}$ Finally
$\begin{aligned} & 6 \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{C}}+12 \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{c}}=30+0 \\ & 18 \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{C}}=30 \\ & \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{30}{18}=\frac{5}{3} \mathrm{Volt} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{q}_1=\frac{6 \times 5}{3}=10 \mu \mathrm{C} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{q}_2=\frac{12 \times 5}{3}=20 \mu \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

=g2+μg32 ; g=10 m s2

B to A

Given: $\mathrm{R}=0.082$ bar $\mathrm{L} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$

$\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{\frac{37.8}{108} \times 0.082 \times 500}{1}=14.35 \text { bar } \\
& 2 \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4+\mathrm{O}_2
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{lll}\mathrm{t}=0 & 14.35 \\ \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{eq} & 14.35-2 \mathrm{P} & 2 \mathrm{P}\end{array}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}_{\text {Total }} \text { at eqb }=14.35+\mathrm{P}=18.65 \\ & \mathrm{P}=4.3 \\ & \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5}=5.75 \text { bar } \\ & \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4}=8.6 \mathrm{bar} \\ & \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_2}=4.3 \text { bar } \\ & \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{(8.6)^2 \times(4.3)}{(5.75)^2}=9.619=\mathrm{x} \times 10^{-2} \\ & \mathrm{x}=961.9 \approx 962\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Consider the following electrochemical cell : $\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})(1 \mathrm{bar})\right| \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \| \mathrm{M}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}, 1.0 \mathrm{M}) \mid \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{~s})$ The pH above which, oxygen gas would start to evolve at anode is $\_\_\_\_$ (nearest integer).
$\left[\begin{array}{ll}\text { Given: } & \mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}}{ }_{\mathrm{M}^{2+} / \mathrm{M}}=0.994 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}}{ }_{\mathrm{O}_{2} / \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}=1.23 \mathrm{~V}\end{array}\right\}$ standard reduction potential $]$
questionIds.solution:
In this electrochemical cell, at the anode oxygen evolution occurs from water oxidation. Using Nernst equation for the O₂/H₂O couple: $E_{anode} = E°_{O_2/H_2O} - \frac{0.059}{4} \log \frac{1}{[H^+]^4} = 1.23 - 0.059 \times pH$

At the cathode: $E_{cathode} = 0.994$ V (standard potential for M²⁺/M reduction)

For oxygen to start evolving, the cell potential becomes zero: $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode} = 0$

$0.994 = 1.23 - 0.059 \times pH$

$0.059 \times pH = 0.236$

$pH = 4.0$

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: Which one of the following pairs is an example of polar molecular solids?
questionIds.solution:
SO2 is a bent molecule with a central sulfur atom bonded to two oxygen atoms. The oxygen atoms are more electronegative than sulfur, causing a separation of charge. This results in a bent molecular geometry and a net dipole moment, making SO2 a polar molecule.

NH3 is a trigonal pyramidal molecule with a central nitrogen atom bonded to three hydrogen atoms. Again, the electronegativity difference between nitrogen and hydrogen leads to a separation of charge. The arrangement of the atoms in NH3 gives it a net dipole moment, making it a polar molecule.

As density of the solutions are same
So $\frac{5.25}{M}=\frac{15}{60}$
$M=\frac{5.25 \times 60}{1.5}=210$
Hence (D) is correct

The Lyman series lies in the ultraviolet, in chemistry, the Lyman series is a hydrogen spectral series of transitions and resulting ultraviolet emission lines of the hydrogen atom as an electron goes from n≥2 to n=1  (where n is the principal quantum number), the lowest energy level of the electron.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: Amongst the following, the total number of compounds soluble in aqueous $NaOH$ at room temperature is:
questionIds.solution:
To determine which compounds are soluble in aqueous $NaOH$, we need to identify the compounds that are sufficiently acidic to react with $NaOH$ (a strong base) to form water-soluble sodium salts. Generally, carboxylic acids and phenols are acidic enough to dissolve in aqueous $NaOH$, whereas alcohols, aldehydes, amines, and hydrocarbons are not.

Let us analyze each compound:
(I) Benzaldehyde: It is an aldehyde. It does not have an acidic proton and is insoluble in aqueous $NaOH$.
(II) 1-Naphthol: It is a phenol derivative. Phenols are acidic and react with $NaOH$ to form soluble phenoxide salts. (Soluble)
(III) 4-(Dimethylamino)phenol: It contains a phenolic $-OH$ group. Despite the basic amino group, the acidic phenol group will react with $NaOH$ to form a soluble salt. (Soluble)
(IV) p-Cresol: It is a phenol derivative. It reacts with $NaOH$ to form a soluble sodium salt. (Soluble)
(V) Benzoic acid: It is a carboxylic acid, which is highly acidic and readily dissolves in aqueous $NaOH$. (Soluble)
(VI) N,N-Dimethylcyclohexanamine: It is an aliphatic amine. It is basic in nature and insoluble in aqueous $NaOH$.
(VII) 1-Naphthoic acid: It is a carboxylic acid and will dissolve in aqueous $NaOH$. (Soluble)
(VIII) 1,4-Di-tert-butylbenzene: It is a hydrocarbon. It is neutral and insoluble in aqueous $NaOH$.
(IX) 1-Naphthalenemethanol: It is an alcohol (benzyl-type). Alcohols are generally not acidic enough to react with aqueous $NaOH$. (Insoluble)

The compounds soluble in aqueous $NaOH$ are (II), (III), (IV), (V), and (VII).
Total number of soluble compounds = $5$.

Answer: $5$

questionIds.q:

A. Vapour pressure of the solution is less than that of pure solvent
B. Vapour pressure of the solution is more than that of pure solvent
C. Only solute molecules solidify at the freezing point
D. Only solvent molecules solidify at the freezing point
questionIds.solution:
Since the solute particles do not evaporate, the vapour pressure of the solution is lower than that of the pure solvent. The lowering of the vapour pressure depends on the number of solute particles that have been dissolved.

In the depression in freezing point experiment, it is found that the vapour pressure of a solution is less than that of pure solvent. Only solvent molecules solidify at freezing point.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Two women and some men participated in a chess tournament in which every participant played two games with each of the other participants. If the number of games that the men played between them-selves exceeds the number of games that the men played with the women by<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mn>66</mn></math>, then the number of men who participated in the tournament lies in the interval
questionIds.solution:
Let the number of men participating in tournament =m.

Number of women participating in tournament =2.

Each player will play 2 games with every other player and two women will play 2 games with each man.

Total numbers of games played by women =2×2m=4m

Total number of games played by men =2·mC2

From given data

2· mC2-4m=66

⇒2mm-12-4m=66

⇒m2-5m-66=0

⇒m-11m+6=0

⇒m=11 or -6 but m∈N, m≠-6

⇒m∈10,12.

questionIds.solution:
Common Difference of first series =4

Common difference of second series =7

First common term =23

Common difference =L.C.M7×4=28

To find number of common terms we put

Last term ≤407

⇒23+n-1×28≤407

⇒n-1×28≤384

⇒n≤13.71+1

⇒n≤14.71

⇒n=14

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q:
If $\int \frac{\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}+x\right)^{10}}{\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}-x\right)^9} d x=$
$\frac{1}{m}\left(\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}+x\right)^n\left(n \sqrt{1+x^2}-x\right)\right)+C \text { where } C$
is the constant of integration and $m, n \in N$, then $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to
questionIds.solution:
rationalise
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \int \frac{\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}+x\right)^{10}}{\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}-x\right)^9} \times \frac{\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}+x\right)^9}{\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}+x\right)^9} d x \\ & \Rightarrow \int \frac{\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}+x\right)^{19}}{1} d x
\end{aligned}$
Put $\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}+\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{t}$
$\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}}+1\right) \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{dt}$
$\mathrm{dx}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}} \sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}$
Now as $\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}+\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{t}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { so } \sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}-\mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \\ & \therefore \sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{t}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}\right)
\end{aligned}$
Thus $I=\int \mathrm{t}^{19} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{t}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}\right)$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \int\left(\mathrm{t}^{19}+\mathrm{t}^{17}\right) \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{t}^{20}}{20}+\frac{\mathrm{t}^{18}}{18}\right)+\mathrm{C} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{t}^{19}}{360}\left[9 \mathrm{t}+\frac{10}{\mathrm{t}}\right]+\mathrm{C} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{t}^{19}}{360}\left[9\left(\mathrm{t}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}\right)+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}\right]+\mathrm{C} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\left(\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}+\mathrm{x}\right)^{19}}{360}\left[9\left(2 \sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}\right)+\left(\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}-\mathrm{x}\right)\right]+\mathrm{C} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\left(\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}+\mathrm{x}\right)^{19}}{360}\left[19 \sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}-\mathrm{x}\right]+\mathrm{C} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{~m}=360, \mathrm{n}=19 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{n}=379
\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If the mean of the data: $7,8,9,7,8,7, \lambda, 8$ is 8 , then the variance of this data is
questionIds.solution:
$$
\text { } \begin{aligned}
&\bar{x}=\frac{7+8+9+7+8+7+\lambda+8}{8}=8 \\
&\Rightarrow \frac{54+\lambda}{8}=8 \Rightarrow \lambda=10
\end{aligned}
$$
Now variance $=\sigma^2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&(7-8)^2+(8-8)^2+(9-8)^2+(7-8)^2+(8-8)^2 \\
&=\frac{+(7-8)^2+(10-8)^2+(8-8)^2}{8} \\
&\Rightarrow \sigma^2=\frac{1+0+1+1+0+1+4+0}{8}=\frac{8}{8}=1 \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence, the variance is 1 .

Now, a second ball is drawn at random from it. The probability that the second ball is red, is:
questionIds.solution:
Let A is event that 2nd drawn ball is red.

∴PA=PR×PA/R+PG×P A/G

=57×47+27×67

=3249

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let $\vec{a}=\hat{i}-\hat{k}, \vec{b}=x \hat{i}+\hat{j}+(1-x) \hat{k}$ and $\vec{c}=y \hat{i}+x \hat{j}+(1+x-y) \hat{k}$. Then $[\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}]$ depends on
questionIds.solution:
$\vec{a}=\hat{i}-\hat{k}, \vec{b}=x \hat{i}+\hat{j}+(1-x) \hat{k}$ and $\vec{c}=y \hat{i}+x \hat{j}+(1+x-y) \hat{k}$
$[\vec{a} \vec{b} \vec{c}]=\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \vec{b} \times \vec{c}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\
x & 1 & 1-x \\
y & x & 1+x-y
\end{array}\right|=\hat{i}\left(1+x-x-x^2\right)-\hat{j}\left(x+x^2-x y-y+x y\right)+\hat{k}\left(x^2-y\right) \\
& \vec{a} .(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=1
\end{aligned}
$$
which does not depend on $\mathrm{x}$ and $\mathrm{y}$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: All the students of a class performed poorly in Mathematics. The teacher decided to give grace marks of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> to each of the students. Which of the following statistical measures will not change even after the grace marks were given ?
questionIds.solution:
As we know that with the change in origin, the variance does not change.

Therefore, variance does not change even after giving grace marks of 10 to each of the students.

Whereas, mean, mode and median changes upon changing the origin.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Minimum number of times a fair coin must be tossed so that the probability of getting at least one head is more than 99%  is:
questionIds.solution:
Let coin is tossed n times.

The probability of getting a head in a single throw of a die is 12.

By using binomial theorem, we know that the probability that an event X appear r times in n trials is PX=r=Crnpn-rqr, q=1-p, r=0, 1, 2, ..., n.

Now, P(at least are head)=1-P(no head)

=1-12n

Using, the given condition, we get

1-12n≥0.99

⇒12n≤1-0.99

⇒12n≤1100

As, 26=64 and 27=128, hence, the minimum value of n is 7.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: The square of the distance of the point $(-2, -8, 6)$ from the line $\dfrac{x-1}{1} = \dfrac{y-1}{2} = \dfrac{z}{-1}$ along the line $\dfrac{x+5}{1} = \dfrac{y+5}{-1} = \dfrac{z}{2}$ is equal to:
questionIds.solution:
Let the given point be $P(-2, -8, 6)$.

The distance is measured along the line $\dfrac{x+5}{1} = \dfrac{y+5}{-1} = \dfrac{z}{2}$, which has direction ratios $(1, -1, 2)$.

The equation of the line passing through $P$ and parallel to this line is:
$\dfrac{x+2}{1} = \dfrac{y+8}{-1} = \dfrac{z-6}{2} = \lambda$

Any point on this line can be written as $Q(\lambda - 2, -\lambda - 8, 2\lambda + 6)$.

Since $Q$ lies on the line $\dfrac{x-1}{1} = \dfrac{y-1}{2} = \dfrac{z}{-1}$, we substitute the coordinates of $Q$ into its equation:
$\dfrac{\lambda - 2 - 1}{1} = \dfrac{-\lambda - 8 - 1}{2} = \dfrac{2\lambda + 6}{-1}$

$\dfrac{\lambda - 3}{1} = \dfrac{-\lambda - 9}{2} = \dfrac{2\lambda + 6}{-1}$

Taking the first two parts:
$2(\lambda - 3) = -\lambda - 9$
$2\lambda - 6 = -\lambda - 9$
$3\lambda = -3 \Rightarrow \lambda = -1$

Substituting $\lambda = -1$ into the coordinates of $Q$, we get:
$Q(-1 - 2, -(-1) - 8, 2(-1) + 6) \equiv Q(-3, -7, 4)$

The square of the distance between $P(-2, -8, 6)$ and $Q(-3, -7, 4)$ is:
$PQ^2 = (-3 - (-2))^2 + (-7 - (-8))^2 + (4 - 6)^2$
$PQ^2 = (-1)^2 + (1)^2 + (-2)^2$
$PQ^2 = 1 + 1 + 4 = 6$

Answer: $6$

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let a, b, c be three distinct real numbers, none equal to one. If the vectors ai^+j^+k^, i^+bj^+k^ and i^+j^+ck^ are coplanar, then 11-a+11-b+11-c is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Given that the vectors are coplanar.

That means the determinant is equal to zero.

⇒a111b111c=0

⇒R1→R1–R2 and R2→R2–R3

⇒a-11-b00b-11-c11c=0

Expand the determinant along C1.

⇒(a-1) [c(b-1)-(1-c)]+1[(1-b) (1-c)]=0

⇒c(a-1) (b-1)-(a-1) (1-c)+(1-b) (1-c)=0

⇒c(1-a) (1-b)+(1-a) (1-c)+(1-b) (1-c)+1-a1-b1-c=1-a1-b1-c

⇒(1-a) (1-b)+(1-a) (1-c)+(1-b) (1-c)=1-a1-b1-c

⇒11-a+11-b+11-c=1

Hence this is the required option.

questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mo>[</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>]</mo><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi></math> be given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="" open="{"><mrow><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>m</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable><mo>.</mo></mrow></mfenced></math>If the area bounded by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> sq units, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi>A</mi></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Given f:[-3,1]→R, fx=min(x+6), x2,-3≤x≤0maxx, x2, 0≤x≤1

We have x+6=x2

⇒x2-x-6=0

⇒x-3x+2=0

⇒x=3 & -2.

Also, x=x2

⇒x2-x=0

⇒xx32-1=0

⇒x=0 & x=1.

Hence, the region bounded by fx is given by the following graph.

Hence, the area bounded by y=f(x) and x-axis is

A=∫-3-2(x+6)dx+∫-20x2dx+∫01xdx

⇒A=x22+6x-3-2+x33-20+2x32301

⇒A=42-12-92-18+0+83+2×1-03

⇒A=-10+272+83+23

⇒A=416 sq units.

⇒6A=41.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo><</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mi>y</mi></mfenced><mo><</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, then the sum to infinity of the following series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo></math> is
questionIds.solution:
Let S=x+y+x2+xy+y2+x3+x2y+xy2+y3+.....

Multiply x-y both sides

⇒x-yS=x-y(x+y+x2+xy+y2+x3+x2y+xy2+y3+...)

⇒x-yS=x-yx+y+x-yx2+xy+y2+x-yx3+x2y+xy2+y3+.....

⇒x-yS=x2-y2+x3-y3+x4-y4+.....

⇒x-yS=x2+x3+x4+....-y2+y3+y4+.....

S=1x-yx21-x-y21-y=1x-yx2-x2y-y2+xy2(1-x)(1-y)

S=x+y-xy(1-x)(1-y)

questionIds.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> have a binomial distribution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> such that the sum and the product of the mean and variance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>128</mn></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>X</mi><mo>></mo><mi>n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>k</mi><msup><mn>2</mn><mi>n</mi></msup></mfrac></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to
questionIds.solution:
Let M=Mean and V=Variance M>V

So, M+V=24, MV=128

⇒M=16 and V=8

Now, mean np=16

and variance npq=8⇒q=12

∴  p=12,n=32

Now PX>n-3=12nCn-2n+Cn-1n+Cnn

∴ k2n=12nC3032+C3132+C3232

⇒k=32×312+32+1

=496+33=529

Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> is equal to ______.

questionIds.subject: Mathematics
questionIds.q: Let $\mathrm{P}(4,4 \sqrt{3})$ be a point on the parabola $y^2=4 \mathrm{a} x$ and PQ be a focal chord of the parabola. If M and $N$ are the foot of perpendiculars drawn from $P$ and $Q$ respectively on the directrix of the parabola, then the area of the quadrilateral PQMN is equal to :
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & (4,4 \sqrt{3}) \text { lies on } y^2=4 \mathrm{ax} \\ & \Rightarrow 48=4 \mathrm{a} \cdot 4 \\ & \quad 4 \mathrm{a}=12 \end{aligned}$ $\Rightarrow y^2=12 x$ is equation of parabola Now, parameter of $P$ is $t_1=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow$ Parameters of $Q$ is $\mathrm{t}_2=-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Q}\left(\frac{9}{4},-3 \sqrt{3}\right)$ Area of trapezium PQNM $\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{MN} \cdot(\mathrm{PM}+\mathrm{QN}) \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{MN} \cdot(\mathrm{PS}+\mathrm{QS}) \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{MN} \cdot \mathrm{PQ} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} 7 \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{49}{4}=(343) \frac{\sqrt{3}}{8} \end{aligned}$

questionIds.q: If for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><msup><mi>x</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><msup><mi>x</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mo>…</mo></math>upto <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∞</mo></math> terms and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>+</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>+</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mrow><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> then the ordered pair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to

2+4+6+…+2y3+6+9+…+3y=4log10x

21+2+3+----y31+2+3+----y=4log10x

log10x=6

x=106

Now

So, x, y=106, 9

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A rectangular loop has a sliding connector PQ of length $\ell$ and resistance $\mathrm{R} \Omega$ and it is moving with a speed $v$ as shown. The set-up is placed in a uniform magnetic field going into the plane of the paper. The three currents $I_1, I_2$ and $I$ are
questionIds.solution: A moving conductor is equivalent to a battery of emf $=\mathrm{v} \mathrm{B} \ell \quad$ (motion emf) Equivalent circuit $$ \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{l}_1+\mathrm{l}_2 $$ applying Kirchoff's law $$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}_1 R+I R-v B \ell=0 \\ & \mathrm{I}_2 R+I R-v B \ell=0 \end{aligned} $$ adding (1) \& (2) $$ \begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{IR}+\mathrm{IR}=2 \mathrm{vB} \ell \\ & \mathrm{I}=\frac{2 \mathrm{vB} \ell}{3 \mathrm{R}} \\ & \mathrm{I}_1=\mathrm{I}_2=\frac{\mathrm{vB} \ell}{3 \mathrm{R}} \end{aligned} $$

f = 60cm

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion $\mathrm{A}$ and the other is labelled as Reason $\mathrm{R}$ Assertion A : The kinetic energy needed to project a body of mass m from earth surface to infinity is $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mgR}$, where R is the radius of earth.
Reason $\mathrm{R}$ : The maximum potential energy of a body is zero when it is projected to infinity from earth surface.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the option given below
options: Both $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ are true and $\mathrm{R}$ is the correct explanation of $\mathrm{A}$
(4) Both $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ are true but $\mathrm{R}$ is NOT the correct explanation of $\mathrm{A}$
questionIds.solution:
$\mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{Gm}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)=\mathrm{mgR}$
Assertion wrong
at $\infty$
$\mathrm{U}=0$
$\therefore$ Reason correct.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The time taken by an object to slide down <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> rough inclined plane is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times as it takes to slide down a perfectly smooth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> incline plane. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the object and the incline plane is:
questionIds.solution:
Let angle of inclination is θ=45°.

Then, acceleration along the incline in case of smooth incline will be, a1=g sinθ=g2.

In case of rough incline, kinetic friction will act against the direction of motion. Normal force acting on the object will be mgcosθ. Therefore, acceleration along the incline in case of rough incline will be,

a2=g sinθ-μg cosθ=g2-μg2.

Now as per question,

t2=nt1

and as the displacement covered is the same in both case, 0+12a1t12=0+12a2t22

⇒g2t12=g2-μg2n2t12

⇒μ=1-1n2

(Round off to the nearest integer)
questionIds.solution:
n=0.60=1-TLTH

TLTH=0.4⇒TL=0.4×400

=160 K

=-113°C

3mgsin⁡θ=μ3mgcos⁡θ

questionIds.q: In an experiment to verify Newton's law of cooling. a graph is plotted between. the temperature difference ΔT of the water and surroundings and time as shown in figure. The initial temperature of water is taken as 80°C. The value of t2 as mentioned in the graph will be
questionIds.solution:
According to the Newton's law of cooling, -dTdt=KT-T0.

Applying approximation, -∆T∆t=KT1+T22-T0 

For first 6 min, T1=80°C, T2=40+20=60°C, T0=20°C

⇒-60-806=K70-20⇒K=-60-806×70-20

⇒K=206×50=115

For 6 to t2, T1=60°C, T2=20+20=40°C, T0=20°C

-60-80t2-6=K50-20⇒t2-6=15×2030⇒t2=16 min

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A diatomic molecule is made of two masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ which are separated by a distance $r$. If we calculate its rotational energy by applying Bohr's rule of angular momentum quantization, its energy will be given by ( $n$ is an integer)
questionIds.solution:
$r_1=\frac{m_2 r}{m_1+m_2} ; r_2=\frac{m_1 r}{m_1+m_2}$
$\left(l_1+l_2\right) \omega=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}=nh$
$K . E=\frac{1}{2}\left(l_1+l_2\right) \omega^2=\frac{n^2 h^2\left(m_1+m_2\right)}{2 m_1 m_2 r^2}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: Two cars are approaching each other at an equal speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>hr</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> When they see each other, both blow horns having a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>676</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The beat frequency heard by each driver will be _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> [Velocity of sound in air is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>340</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
questionIds.solution:
Frequency of sound heard by car-1, which comes by reflection from car-2

f1=f0340+2340-2340+2340-2

=f03423382

Frequency of sound coming directly from car-2

f2=f0340+2340-2

∴  f1-f2=f0342338342338-1=8.09≃8 Hz

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The refractive index of the material of a concave lens is $\mu$. It is immersed in a medium of refractive index $\mu_1$. A parallel beam of light is incident on the lens. The path of the emergent rays when $\mu_1>\mu$ is:
questionIds.solution:
If a lens of refractive index $\mu$ is immersed in a medium of refractive index $\mu_1$, then its focal length in medium is given by
$$
\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{m}}}=\left({ }_{\mathrm{m}} \mu_l-1\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}\right)-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right)
$$
If $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}$ is the focal length of lens in air, then
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}}=\left({ }_{\mathrm{a}} \mu_l-1\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right)\right. \\
&\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}}=\frac{\left({ }_{\mathrm{a}} \mu_l-1\right)}{\left({ }_{\mathrm{m}} \mu_l-1\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$
If $\mu_1>\mu$, then $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{m}}$ and $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}$ have opposite signs and the nature of lens changes i.e. a convex lens diverges the light rays and concave lens converges the light rays. Thus given option (a) is correct.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A system of two blocks of masses m=2 kg and M=8 kg is placed on a smooth table as shown in figure. The coefficient of static friction between two blocks is 0.5. The maximum horizontal force F that can be applied to the block of mass M so that the blocks move together will be g=9.8 m s-2
questionIds.solution:
Maximum acceleration of the upper block will be,

amax=μg=0.5×9.8=4.9 m s-2

Therefore, maximum force that can be applied on the lower block is,

Fmax=2+8×4.9=49 N

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A circular disc has radius $R_{1}$ and thickness $T_{1}$. Another circular disc made of the same material has radius $R_{2}$ and thickness $T_{2}$. If the moment of inertia of both discs are same and $\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=2$ then $\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\frac{1}{\alpha}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
questionIds.solution:
For a circular disc made of material with density $\rho$, moment of inertia is $I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho \pi R^2 T \cdot R^2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho \pi R^4 T$.
For disc 1: $I_1 = \frac{1}{2}\rho \pi R_1^4 T_1$
For disc 2: $I_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho \pi R_2^4 T_2$
Given $I_1 = I_2$:
$R_1^4 T_1 = R_2^4 T_2$
With $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = 2$:
$(2R_2)^4 T_1 = R_2^4 T_2$
$16 R_2^4 T_1 = R_2^4 T_2$
$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{16}$
Therefore $\alpha = 16$.

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The heat absorbed by a system in going through the given cyclic process is :
questionIds.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{U}=0(\text { Cyclic process }) \\ & \Delta \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{W}=\text { area of } \mathrm{P}-\mathrm{V} \text { curve. } \\ & =\pi \times\left(140 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~Pa}\right) \times\left(140 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^3\right) \\ & \Delta \mathrm{Q}=61.6 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: A light ray is incident perpendicular to one face of a $90^{\circ}$ prism and is totally internally reflected at the glass-air interface. If the angle of reflection is $45^{\circ}$, we conclude that the refractive index $n$

Angle of incidence $\mathrm{i}>\mathrm{C}$ for total internal reflection. Here $\mathrm{i}=45^{\circ}$ inside the medium.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore 45^{\circ}>\sin ^{-1}(1 / n) \\
& \Rightarrow n>\sqrt{ } 2 .
\end{aligned}
$$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: As shown in the figure, two infinitely long, identical wires are bent by 90o and placed in such a way that the segments LP and QM are along the x - axis, while segments PS and QN are parallel to the y - axis. If OP=OQ=4cm, and the magnitude of the magnetic field at O is 10-4 T, and the two wires carry equal currents (see figure), the magnitude of the current in each wire and the direction of the magnetic field at O will be μ0=4π×10-7NA-2:
questionIds.solution:
As we know that the magnetic field due to a infinite wire at a distance of d is given as

but there are two wires so that the net magnetic field is given as

10-4=24π×10-7×i4π×4100

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The half life of a radioactive substance is 20 minutes. The approximate time interval $\left(t_2-t_1\right)$ between the time $t_2$ when $\frac{2}{3}$ of it has decayed and time $t_1$ and $\frac{1}{3}$ of it had decayed is :
questionIds.solution:
$\mathrm{t}_{\frac{1}{2}}=20$ minutes
$\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}_2} \quad \lambda \mathrm{t}_1=\ln 3$
$\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{~N}_0=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}_2} \mathrm{t}_1=\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln 3$
$\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{~N}_0=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda t_2}$
$\mathrm{t}_2=\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln \frac{3}{2}$
$\mathrm{t}_2-\mathrm{t}_1=\frac{1}{\lambda}\left[\ln \frac{3}{2}-\ln 3\right]$
$=\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln \left[\frac{1}{2}\right]=\frac{0.693}{\lambda}$
$=20 \min$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: An ideal monatomic gas with pressure $P$, volume $V$ and temperature $T$ is expanded isothermally to a volume $2 V$ and a final pressure $P_i$. If the same gas is expanded adiabatically to a volume $2 \mathrm{~V}$, the final pressure is $P_a$. The ratio $\frac{P_a}{P_i}$ is
questionIds.solution:
For isothermal process :
$$
\begin{aligned}
& P V=P_i 2 V \\
& P=2 P_i
\end{aligned}
$$
For adiabatic process
$$
\mathrm{PV}^\gamma=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{a}}(2 \mathrm{~V})^\gamma
$$
$(\because$ for monatomic gas $\gamma=5 / 3)$
[From (i) ]
or, $\quad 2 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{V}^{\frac{5}{3}}=P_a(2 \mathrm{~V})^{\frac{5}{3}}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{P_a}{P_i}=\frac{2}{2^{\frac{5}{3}}} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{P_a}{P_i}=2^{\frac{-2}{3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

questionIds.subject: Physics
questionIds.q: The positive feedback is required by an amplifier to act as an oscillator. The feedback here means
options: External input is necessary to sustain ac signal in output.
A portion of the output power is returned back to the input.
Feedback can be achieved by LR network.
The base-collector junction must be forward biased.
questionIds.solution: When the amplifier connects with positive feedback, it acts as the oscillator. The feedback employed here is positive feedback, this means some amount of the output voltage is given to the input. Hence, the circuit continues its process without any input to the oscillator. The positive feedback is also known as regenerative feedback because the system sustains or regenerates itself without any input source. Hence, the oscillator is nothing but an amplifier with the positive feedback.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: If Z is the compressibility factor, then Van der Waal's equation at low pressure can be written as:
questionIds.solution:
Van der Waal's Equation of state for one mole

P + a V 2 V - b = RT

At low pressure, the characteristic constant (a) becomes significant whereas (b) becomes negligible. So (b) is ignored.

P + a V 2 V = RT

PV + a V = RT

PV RT = RT RT - a VRT = 1 - a VRT

At low pressure for most gases z < 1.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questionIds.q:
Given below are two statements.

Statement I: During electrolytic refining. blister copper deposits precious metals.

Statement II: In the process of obtaining pure copper by electrolysis method, copper blister is used to make the anode.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
questionIds.solution:
Impure Cu obtained from ores is converted to pure Cu in an electrolyte cell that the impure copper as the anode an pure copper as the cathode. The electrolyte is an aqueous solution of CuSO4. At the impure Cu anode, Cu is oxidized along with the more easily oxidized metallic impurities such as Zn and Fe. The less easily oxidized impurities such as Ag,Au, and Pt fall to the bottom of the cell as anode mud, which is reprocessed to recover the precious metals. At the pure Cu cathode, Cu2+ ions get reduced to pure copper metal

Anode (oxidation) : Ms⟶M2+aq+2e-M=Cu,Zn,Fe

Cathode (reduction) : Cu2+aq+2e-⟶Cus

Thus, the net cell reaction simply involves transfer of Cu metal from the impure anode to the pure cathode, Cu obtained by this process is 99.95% pure.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Match List - I with List-II

  List I (Molecule)   List II (Shape)
A. BrF5   i. T-shape
B. H2O ii. See saw
C. ClF3 iii. Bent
D. SF4 iv. Square pyramidal
questionIds.solution:
(A) The steric number of the central Bromine atom is =5 bond pairs+one lone pair =6. Hence the shape is square pyramidal structure and the hybridisation is sp3d2.

(B) H2O molecular geometry is bent because it has two bound atoms and two lone pairs on oxygen, the central atom. The two lone pairs on the oxygen atom push the two hydrogen atoms down further away from the oxygen atom and closer to each other, creating a bent shape and 104.5 degrees angle between the two H atoms.

(C) The molecular geometry of ClF3 is said to be T-shaped. ClF3 acquires such a shape because two lone pairs take up equatorial positions as they require more space and greater repulsions. They are arranged in a trigonal bipyramidal shape with 3 bonds and 2 lone pairs.

(D)  SF4 has an sp3d type of hybridisation. In a molecule of SF4, each S atom is bonded to four fluorine atoms.

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: Oxidation state of sulphur in anions $\mathrm{SO}_3^{2-}, \mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{2-}$ and $\mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_6^{2-}$ increases in the orders :
questionIds.solution:
$\mathrm{In} \mathrm{SO}_3^{--}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x+3(-2)=-2 ; x=+4 \\
& \text { In } \mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{--} \\
& 2 x+4(-2)=-2 \\
& 2 x-8=-2 \\
& 2 x=6 ; \quad x=+3 \\
& \text { In } \mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_6^{2-} \\
& 2 x+6(-2)=-2 \\
& 2 x=10 ; \quad x=+5
\end{aligned}
$$
hence the correct order is
$$
\mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_4{ }^{--} < \mathrm{SO}_3^{--} < \mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_6{ }^{--}
$$

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q: A species having carbon with sextet of electrons and can act as electrophile is called
questionIds.solution:
A carbocation is a molecule in which a carbon atom has a positive charge and three bonds. We can say that, basically, they are carbon cations. Formerly, it was known as carbonium ion. Carbocation today is defined as any even-electron cation that possesses a significant positive charge on the carbon atom.

The carbon cations are very reactive and unstable due to an incomplete octet. In simple words, carbocations do not have eight electrons; therefore, they do not satisfy the octet rule.

In carbocation, the hybridisation of carbon will be sp2 and its shape is trigonal planar. There is also a vacant p- orbital which indicates its electron-deficient nature. The carbon has 6 electrons in its valence shell. Due to this, it is an electron-deficient species, also known as an electrophile.

Six electron species

questionIds.subject: Chemistry
questionIds.q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : Aqueous solution of ammonium carbonate is basic.
Statement (II) : Acidic/basic nature of salt solution of a salt of weak acid and weak base depends on Ka and Kb value of acid and the base forming it.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
questionIds.solution:
Aqueous solution of (NH4)2CO3 is Basic.

pH of salt of weak acid and weak base depends on Ka and Kb value of acid and the base forming it.

Solution is basic if

Kb (weak base) >Ka (weak acid).

Ka (HCO3-)=4.3×10−13

Kb(NH4OH)=1.8×10−5

Kb (Ammonium hydroxide) >Ka (Hydrogen carbonate)

∴ Hence, solution of Ammonium carbonate is basic in nature.

Hence Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.

BeOH2 dissolve in alkali due to it's amphoteric nature.
